10568-100094.overview: This technical report presents a comprehensive assessment of how climate change impacts human health and nutrition in the Philippines, specifically examining the effects of typhoons, floods, and drought on food security and nutritional status of vulnerable populations. The study analyzes cross-sectional data from national nutrition surveys (2013 and 2015) merged with climate exposure data to understand relationships between extreme weather events and nutrition outcomes.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a technical research report presenting original analysis of survey data with statistical modeling and findings

classification.development_stage_evidence: Study utilized cross-sectional data from the 2013 and 2015 National Nutrition Surveys conducted nationwide by the Department of Science and Technology- Food and Nutrition Research Institute (DOST-FNRI)

who_its_for.primary_audience: Researchers, policymakers, and development practitioners working on climate change adaptation and nutrition programs
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and international development organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical archipelago vulnerable to typhoons, floods, and drought

how_it_works.approach_summary: The study merges national nutrition survey data with climate exposure data from multiple sources to analyze associations between extreme weather events and nutrition outcomes using logistic regression models.
key_steps: Collect household and individual nutrition data from national surveys
Gather climate exposure data on typhoons, floods, and drought from government and research sources
Merge datasets at provincial and municipal levels based on timing of exposure
Apply statistical models controlling for socioeconomic and demographic factors
Analyze associations between climate exposure and nutrition outcomes
Generate findings on vulnerable populations and time-dependent effects
Provide recommendations for climate adaptation programs
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Analysis covers exposure periods from 6 months prior to survey dates in 2013 and 2015
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: National nutrition survey data, climate disaster records, agricultural production data, household demographic and socioeconomic information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Statistical analysis software (Stata), access to national survey datasets, climate monitoring data
what_youll_need.human_resources: Researchers with expertise in nutrition, epidemiology, and statistical analysis
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with national statistical offices, disaster management agencies, and research institutions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Statistical models showing associations between climate exposure and nutrition outcomes, prevalence estimates by population groups and regions
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Inform climate change adaptation and mitigation programs targeting vulnerable populations, guide policy responses to protect nutrition during disasters
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Study found significant associations between climate exposure and nutrition outcomes, with effects varying by timing and type of exposure

practical_examples.description: Analysis of typhoon and flood exposure effects on household energy intake and child stunting across all regions
practical_examples.key_result: Households exposed to typhoons/floods 3 months prior were 1.54 times more likely to meet recommended energy intake, but those exposed 6 months prior were less likely

practical_examples.description: Drought exposure analysis focusing on agricultural regions using satellite-based monitoring data
practical_examples.key_result: Children in drought-exposed areas had 36% increased odds of stunting, while elderly adults showed complex patterns depending on exposure duration

10568-100094.limitations_and_considerations: The study uses cross-sectional data which limits understanding of continuing effects over time. Cohort data would better capture long-term impacts. The analysis is limited to specific time periods and may not capture all climate-nutrition pathways. Exposure classification relies on administrative boundaries which may not perfectly match actual exposure patterns.
key_takeaways: Socioeconomic status, household size, and food security are key determinants of nutrition outcomes during climate events
Timing of climate exposure matters - effects on nutrition vary significantly between short-term (1-3 months) and longer-term (5-6 months) exposure periods
Agricultural households are more likely to meet energy requirements but children in these households face higher stunting risks during drought
Vulnerable populations (poorest quintile, large households, food insecure) consistently show worse nutrition outcomes during climate events
Climate adaptation programs should prioritize nutrition protection for indigenous peoples, agricultural communities, and households with young children

source_and_citation.full_citation: Duante, Charmaine A.; Austria, Rovea Ernazelle G.; Borigas, John Michael E.; Acuin, Cecilia S. (2018). Technical Report: Assessment of the Impacts of Climate Change on Human Health and Nutrition. CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Document contains extensive statistical tables and technical details that provide comprehensive information about methodology and findings

10568-100095.overview: This is a training workshop report documenting a 3-day capacity building program held in Nairobi in April 2018 to enhance understanding and implementation of agricultural components within Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs) for climate action. The workshop targeted government officials from Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, and Ethiopia to address knowledge gaps about UNFCCC processes and provide practical tools for NDC implementation in agriculture.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a comprehensive training workshop report that includes modules, exercises, and evaluation results, functioning as both documentation and replicable training content

classification.development_stage_evidence: The training was successfully delivered to 12 participants from 4 countries with positive evaluation results, and plans were made for similar trainings in Francophone West Africa

who_its_for.primary_audience: Government officials from ministries of agriculture, environment, finance, and planning
who_its_for.organizational_context: National government ministries, regional organizations like NEPAD, and development agencies working on climate policy implementation

how_it_works.approach_summary: The training consists of eight modules delivered over three days, combining presentations, group discussions, and practical exercises to build understanding of NDCs, climate finance, monitoring systems, and stakeholder engagement in agricultural climate action.
key_steps: Module 1: Introduction to UNFCCC initiatives relevant to agriculture
Module 2: Understanding Nationally Determined Contributions (NDCs)
Module 3: Climate Smart Agriculture for transformative change
Module 4: Analysis and tools for priority setting in agriculture
Module 5: Climate finance (parts 1 and 2)
Module 6: Role of private sector in NDC development and implementation
Module 7: Monitoring NDC implementation (parts 1 and 2)
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 3 days (10-12 April 2018)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Country NDC documents, participant expertise from relevant ministries, presentation materials and facilitator guides

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Presentation materials, flipcharts, post-it notes for group exercises, hard copies of country NDCs
what_youll_need.human_resources: Facilitator, presenters from CCAFS, CGIAR centers, and FAO, participants from relevant government ministries
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership between organizing institutions (NEPAD, CCAFS, GIZ), venue arrangements, travel coordination
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but includes venue costs, participant travel, facilitator fees, and material preparation

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Enhanced capacity among ministry staff for NDC implementation, concrete action plans for next 6-12 months, improved understanding of climate finance and MRV systems
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Better implementation of agricultural components in national climate commitments, improved coordination between ministries, increased private sector engagement
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Positive evaluation results with average scores of 4.0-4.6 out of 5 for training quality, participants identified specific modules they would draw upon most (climate finance, private sector, MRV)

practical_examples.description: Government officials from ministries of agriculture, environment, and finance participated in hands-on exercises to review their countries' NDCs and identify agricultural sector roles
practical_examples.key_result: Participants developed concrete 6-month and 12-month action plans including private sector engagement, MRV system improvements, and inter-ministerial coordination

10568-100095.limitations_and_considerations: Some participants had difficulty getting travel approval leading to incomplete country representation. The indicator review exercise was too complex for the time allocated and needs redesign. Participants suggested more time for discussion, less technical presentations, and more relevant country examples.
key_takeaways: Climate finance, private sector engagement, and MRV systems were identified as the most valuable learning areas by participants
Group exercises reviewing country NDCs were particularly useful as some participants were unfamiliar with their own country's commitments
Key MRV challenges identified were capacity, data, funding, and coordination - with specific solutions proposed for each
Participants committed to concrete actions including greater private sector involvement and improved gender integration in NDCs
The training model is being replicated in other African regions, indicating its effectiveness and scalability

source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

10568-100109.overview: This training manual documents a farmer business school model that links smallholder farmers producing indigenous vegetables to school feeding programs in Busia County, Kenya. The approach addresses malnutrition and limited market access for nutrient-rich indigenous crops by creating sustainable procurement relationships between farmer groups and educational institutions.

resource_type.type_notes: Matches metadata classification and presents a documented model with training components

classification.development_stage_evidence: 25 farmer groups were trained and 14 contracts and tenders were secured with schools and hospitals for the provision of indigenous vegetables

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, agricultural extension workers, school feeding program managers, and farmer group facilitators
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural research organizations, NGOs, government agencies implementing school feeding programs, farmer cooperatives

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Areas suitable for indigenous vegetable production with existing school feeding programs

how_it_works.approach_summary: The model uses a three-pillar framework linking supply-side capacity building for farmers with demand-side awareness raising in schools. Farmer Business Schools train groups on production, marketing, and business management while nutrition education activities promote acceptance of indigenous vegetables in institutional markets.
key_steps: Train farmer groups using adapted Farmer Business School model on production, processing, handling, marketing and financial management
Conduct nutrition education and cooking demonstrations in schools, health centers and communities
Facilitate negotiations and memorandums of understanding between farmer groups and schools
Provide nutritional composition analysis of indigenous vegetables to support marketing
Organize field days and awareness events to promote local foods
Establish monitoring systems to track supply contracts and outcomes
Scale successful partnerships to additional farmer groups and institutions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Six months training period with ongoing supply contracts
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Nutritional composition data of indigenous vegetables, market demand assessment, farmer capacity assessment

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Laboratory facilities for nutritional analysis, training materials adapted to local context
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained facilitators for Farmer Business Schools, nutrition educators, agricultural extension workers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with schools, health facilities, local government support, farmer group organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Costs vary between KES 30-50 per kilogram ($0.30-0.50) for vegetable procurement

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Trained farmer groups, established supply contracts, increased production and marketing of indigenous vegetables
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved nutrition in schools, increased farmer incomes, enhanced food biodiversity conservation, strengthened local food systems
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: 547 farmers trained, 14 contracts secured, network expanded from 400 to 5,500 students served, quantities supplied ranging from 10-128 kg per week per group

practical_examples.description: 25 farmer groups trained to supply indigenous vegetables to schools and health facilities through negotiated contracts
practical_examples.key_result: Network expanded to serve 5,500 pupils with healthy school meals and generated steady income for farmers

10568-100109.limitations_and_considerations: Limited farmer capacity to produce sufficient quantities, water limitations, need for better infrastructure, seasonal supply challenges, requirement for reliable nutritional data, need for stakeholder engagement and communication to avoid mistrust, infrastructure limitations in schools for processing indigenous vegetables
key_takeaways: Farmer Business School model effectively links smallholder farmers to institutional markets for indigenous vegetables
Nutritional data and education are critical for overcoming negative perceptions of indigenous vegetables
Successful implementation requires simultaneous capacity building on both supply and demand sides
Local procurement policies can support biodiversity conservation while improving nutrition outcomes
Scaling requires addressing infrastructure, water access, and stakeholder engagement challenges

10568-100121.overview: A participatory assessment framework designed to help farming communities identify opportunities for diversifying their agroecosystems to strengthen climate change resilience. The framework guides communities through evaluating their current agricultural biodiversity and exploring diversification options at multiple levels - from species and varieties to landscape-scale interventions.

resource_type.type_notes: Clearly described as an assessment method/framework with structured steps and tools

classification.development_stage_evidence: The assessment was piloted in 2017 with communities in Guatemala, India, and Mali

who_its_for.primary_audience: Farming communities, NGOs, extension agents, development practitioners
who_its_for.organizational_context: NGOs, extension services, development organizations working with smallholder farmers

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Diverse agroecosystems including milpa systems, millet-based systems, rice-based systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: A four-step participatory assessment that engages communities to understand climate impacts, evaluate current agricultural diversity, and identify diversification opportunities at species, farm, and landscape levels.
key_steps: Understanding local experiences of climate change through timelines and seasonal calendars
Evaluating species, varieties, and breeds for climate resilience traits
Assessing field and farm diversification practices like intercropping and agroforestry
Evaluating landscape-level land uses for climate buffering and resource provision
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 5.5 hours total workshop time
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Community knowledge of local climate patterns, crop varieties, farming practices, and land uses

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Large sheets of paper, markers, tape for displaying information
what_youll_need.human_resources: Facilitator and notetaker, mixed groups of 16-35 participants (ideally 38-71% women)
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Community engagement and participation willingness
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Basic workshop materials and facilitator time

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Climate change timeline, seasonal calendar, species/variety trait evaluations, cropping system assessments, land use evaluations
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced climate resilience through targeted diversification strategies, improved harvest security, better adaptation to climate variability
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Pilot results showed identification of stress-tolerant varieties, recognition of beneficial farming practices, and priority areas for ecosystem restoration

practical_examples.description: Maya Ch'orti' communities identified sorghum as more drought and pest resistant than maize varieties, leading to seed exchange plans between villages
practical_examples.key_result: Increased awareness of bean varieties with greater climate tolerance

practical_examples.description: Gond farmers recognized millets as more tolerant to unseasonal rains compared to rice, wheat, and maize
practical_examples.key_result: Identified gaps in winter crop frost tolerance and opportunities for millet promotion

practical_examples.description: Communities identified fonio and Bambara groundnut as strategic crops for climate adaptation due to poor soil adaptation and pest tolerance
practical_examples.key_result: Recognition of traditional crops that could be revived for climate resilience

10568-100121.limitations_and_considerations: Barriers include degraded soils, pressure on wild areas, lack of markets for stress-tolerant crops, and limited access to diverse crop varieties. The method requires community willingness to participate and may need gender-specific workshops in some contexts.
key_takeaways: Diversification at multiple levels (species, farm, landscape) is essential for climate resilience
Local communities possess valuable knowledge about climate-adapted varieties and practices
Participatory assessment can reveal both opportunities and barriers for agricultural diversification
Traditional and neglected crops often have important climate resilience traits
Supporting diversification requires addressing market, institutional, and resource access barriers

source_and_citation.full_citation: Mijatović, D., Meldrum, G., and Robitaille, R. (2019) Diversification for Climate Change Resilience: Participatory assessment of opportunities for diversifying agroecosystems. Bioversity International and the Platform for Agrobiodiversity Research, Rome, Italy.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: Bioversity International and Platform for Agrobiodiversity Research

The course covers greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from soils, livestock, and rice systems, along with methodologies for quantification and strategies for developing low-emission production systems.

Day 2: Learning quantification methodologies including in-vitro and static chamber techniques

10568-100136.overview: A case study documenting the collaboration between IITA and Olam coffee company in Uganda's Mt. Elgon region to develop climate-smart agricultural practices for coffee farmers through farmer segmentation and a 'Stepwise' investment pathway approach. The resource provides insights on private sector engagement strategies and lessons learned from implementing climate resilience solutions in coffee supply chains.

resource_type.type_notes: Document explicitly describes itself as a case study focusing on private sector engagement lessons

classification.development_stage_evidence: Stepwise is being piloted on 26 farm trials in 3 districts, with direct implementation led by Olam in Mount Elgon

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, private sector companies, and researchers working on climate-smart agriculture and supply chain resilience
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural companies, development organizations, research institutes, and NGOs engaged in smallholder farmer support

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Coffee growing regions, specifically Arabica coffee production areas like Mt. Elgon

how_it_works.approach_summary: The approach combines farmer segmentation based on resources and entrepreneurship levels with a Stepwise investment pathway that breaks down climate-smart practices into incremental, affordable steps. Farmers are grouped into segments and follow tailored pathways that use income from each step to fund the next practice.
key_steps: Conduct farmer segmentation to understand diversity in resources and entrepreneurship
Develop Stepwise investment pathways tailored to farmer segments
Establish demonstration plots for climate-smart practices
Provide training through farmer field schools
Monitor impact on yield, pest/disease pressure, and soil nutrition
Scale successful approaches to broader farmer groups
Facilitate learning exchanges between different regions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-year implementation with incremental investment steps
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Farmer information system data, on-farm resources assessment, entrepreneurship evaluation, climate and agronomic data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Stepwise App for data collection and farm development plans, demonstration plots, monitoring systems
what_youll_need.human_resources: Field-based coordinators, extensionists, researchers for data collection and monitoring
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership between research institute and private sector company, farmer groups of 25-30 members
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Co-funding arrangement (USAID grant plus private sector investment), fertilizer and other inputs for demonstration

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Farmer segmentation profiles, tailored Stepwise investment pathways, demonstration plots showing incremental improvements, trained farmer groups
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased adoption of climate-smart practices, improved coffee yield and quality, enhanced farmer resilience to climate change, more efficient use of company extension resources
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Demonstration plots show effects on yield, pest & disease pressure, and soil nutrition; farmers gain confidence in implementing practices after seeing results

practical_examples.description: IITA and Olam implemented farmer segmentation and Stepwise approach with 26 farm trials across 3 districts, serving 150 registered farmers per demonstration plot
practical_examples.key_result: Enabled more efficient targeting of fertilizer distribution and extension resources based on farmer characteristics and readiness

10568-100136.limitations_and_considerations: Private sector engagement requires understanding company motivations and timelines which differ from development objectives. Margins are thin for exporters making them hesitant to take risks on experimental practices. Companies need to see business case and return on investment. Scaling requires moving from farm-level to landscape-level focus.
key_takeaways: Farmer segmentation helps companies allocate extension resources more efficiently by matching training to farmers' specific resources and motivations
Stepwise investment pathways make climate-smart practices more affordable by breaking them into incremental steps funded by previous gains
Private sector engagement requires framing projects in terms of company objectives (supply security, cost savings) rather than development goals
Co-funding arrangements help offset company risk aversion while enabling innovation in sustainability practices
Technology solutions like mobile apps can serve as effective entry points for deeper partnerships on climate resilience

source_and_citation.full_citation: International Institute of Tropical Agriculture; Olam (2018). Improving coffee sector Climate-Smart Awareness and decision-making: Key lessons from private sector engagement in the USAID Learning Community for Supply Chain Resilience Uganda.

10568-100137.overview: This case study documents lessons from private sector engagement in the USAID Learning Community for Supply Chain Resilience, focusing on how cocoa companies in Ghana leveraged climate science tools including climate suitability maps and the Climate Smart Cocoa Manual to improve farmer adaptation and decision-making in response to climate change threats.

classification.development_stage_evidence: Companies like ECOM, Touton, and Hershey have integrated CSC Manual recommendations into their training programs with farmers and are conducting validation demos on farmer plots

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, and private sector companies working on climate-smart agriculture and supply chain resilience
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural commodity companies, development organizations, research institutions, and government agencies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Cocoa growing zones with varying climate vulnerability

key_steps: Conduct climate vulnerability mapping using machine learning models
Develop geographically specific climate-smart practice recommendations
Create Climate Smart Cocoa Manual with zone-specific guidance
Engage companies through sector-wide workshops and training sessions
Support companies in integrating tools into their farmer programs
Pilot and validate recommendations through demonstration plots
Monitor adoption and provide ongoing technical support

what_youll_need.human_resources: Climate scientists, agricultural specialists, extension agents, company sustainability teams

expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased adoption of climate-smart practices by cocoa farmers, improved farm productivity and resilience, reduced deforestation
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Companies successfully integrated tools into training programs; ECOM conducting validation demos on 125 farmer plots; Hershey adapting content for CocoaLink digital platform reaching over 2,000 farmers

practical_examples.description: ECOM integrated zone-specific recommendations into their field assistance programs and is conducting validation demos on 125 farmer rehabilitation plots
practical_examples.key_result: Strategic decision-making improved, with company now considering climate suitability when allocating sustainability resources

practical_examples.description: Touton adapted CSC Manual recommendations into their training materials for 7,000 farmers under their sustainability program
practical_examples.key_result: Successfully integrated climate-specific adaptation strategies into existing training curricula

practical_examples.description: Hershey incorporated CSC Manual content into their CocoaLink digital platform to provide location-specific recommendations based on GPS coordinates

key_takeaways: Private sector engagement is most effective when providing concrete, actionable tools rather than just convenings
Zone-specific climate recommendations are more valuable than standardized approaches for targeting company investments
Working through industry organizations like World Cocoa Foundation improves coordination and reduces confusion
Companies need cost-benefit analysis of climate-smart practices to make business cases to farmers
Farmer-friendly materials and financing solutions are critical gaps that need addressing for successful implementation

10568-100142.overview: This resource presents the Stochastic Impact Evaluation (SIE) approach for assessing investment decisions in honey value chains in Kenya. It demonstrates how to use probabilistic modeling and Monte Carlo simulation to evaluate uncertain investment outcomes, identify critical variables, and determine the value of additional information for decision-making in biodiversity-based value chain interventions.

resource_type.type_notes: Presents the SIE approach as a structured decision analysis framework with specific methodology and application steps

how_it_works.approach_summary: The SIE approach uses Monte Carlo simulation to project decision outcomes by incorporating all relevant variables with their uncertainties expressed as probability distributions. It identifies critical uncertainties and calculates the Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) to prioritize which variables are most valuable to measure.
key_steps: Define the decision question clearly
Develop conceptual model with all relevant variables
Conduct baseline survey and stakeholder consultations
Populate model with probability distributions for variables
Run Monte Carlo simulation to project outcomes
Use Partial Least Squares regression to identify important variables
Calculate Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) for critical variables
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 10-year projection period for investment analysis
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Baseline survey data, expert knowledge, probability distributions for costs, benefits, and risk variables

expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved decision-making for agricultural interventions under uncertainty, reduced chance of project failure, cost-effective measurement campaigns
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully identified honey price as most critical variable in Witu (24% chance of negative outcomes) and demonstrated low risk in Awer (4.6% chance of negative outcomes)

practical_examples.description: Applied SIE to assess honey value chain investment for community with limited beekeeping experience
practical_examples.key_result: Median projected income of Ksh 65,000 per farmer with 24% chance of loss; honey price identified as most critical variable

practical_examples.description: Applied SIE to assess investment in community with established traditional beekeeping
practical_examples.key_result: Median projected income of Ksh 130,000 per farmer with only 4.6% chance of loss; no additional information needed to support investment decision

Value of Information analysis can substantially reduce costs of data collection by prioritizing only decision-relevant measurements
Tailoring interventions to local contexts is critical - different communities require different investment strategies
Clear definition of the decision question is essential and should receive significant attention in the planning process
The approach provides a clear business case for or against investment in projects with uncertain outcomes

10568-100144.overview: This academic paper analyzes the potential and challenges of farmer-led irrigation in sub-Saharan Africa, examining how small-scale, farmer-initiated irrigation systems can increase agricultural productivity and incomes while highlighting significant equity and sustainability concerns that must be addressed for inclusive growth.

classification.development_stage_evidence: Nearly a decade of research in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia points to the broad, positive effects of farmer-led irrigation on income, poverty alleviation and employment

what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Rural finance institutions, technology supply chains, water governance institutions, regulatory frameworks

expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved incomes, poverty alleviation, employment generation, enhanced nutrition, greater resilience to seasonal weather variability and climatic shocks
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Research shows potential for doubling or tripling crop yields and generating additional net household revenues for millions of people

10568-100144.limitations_and_considerations: Current trends largely benefit young, better-off male farmers while women and resource-poor farmers face barriers including high investment costs, limited financing, weak land rights, poor market integration, and inadequate information services. Uncontrolled expansion can lead to water resource degradation, soil contamination, and conflicts over shared resources. Lack of institutions for collective natural resource management restricts access for resource-poor farmers.
key_takeaways: Farmer-led irrigation has significant potential to improve productivity and incomes but current growth patterns are inequitable, primarily benefiting wealthier male farmers
Key barriers include high upfront costs, limited access to appropriate financing, underdeveloped supply chains, and inadequate information services
Women face particular disadvantages due to limited decision-making power, restricted access to technologies and credit, and high labor requirements
Sustainability risks include groundwater depletion, water pollution from agrochemicals, and lack of collective resource management institutions
Achieving inclusive growth requires contextualised interventions addressing finance, supply chains, gender equity, environmental sustainability, and institutional governance

practical_examples.key_result: Number of farmers growing finger millet increased from 42 in 2007 to 76 in 2018, with substantial uptake of improved agronomic practices

10568-100157.overview: This policy brief presents recommendations for establishing Open Source Seed Systems (OSSS) in Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania to enhance climate resilience and food security. It addresses the gap between formal seed systems that serve only 20% of farmers' needs and farmer-managed seed systems that lack policy support, proposing policy reforms to enable free access, use, and exchange of plant genetic materials.

Develop policies facilitating functional open source seed systems
Establish farmer-inclusive intellectual property rights regimes
Create institutional coordination mechanisms
Promote research and development for OSSS
Mobilize resources for OSSS development

10568-100158.overview: This literature review examines behavior change scale-up processes in Market Systems Development (MSD) programs, analyzing how promising interventions and institutional innovations in market systems become accepted and used by large numbers of people. resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive literature review with theoretical framework development

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on market systems development
who_its_for.organizational_context: Development organizations, research institutions, donor agencies implementing MSD programs

It synthesizes literature to develop a six-domain model covering Design, Perceptions, Business Model, Relationships, Communications, and Capacity.
key_steps: Conduct desk-based literature review of MSD, diffusion of innovations, and behavior change fields
Identify scale-up strategies used by MSD programs
Apply DOI theory framework to assess relevance of factors
Categorize findings into six domains with 13 key factors
Compare MSD practices with DOI components to identify gaps
Develop recommendations for future research and practice
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Peer reviewed papers, case studies, evaluation reports, grey literature from MSD programs showing evidence of scale-up

what_youll_need.human_resources: Researchers with expertise in market systems development, diffusion of innovations theory, and behavior change science

practical_examples.key_result: Achieved scale across multiple agricultural input and service sectors

10568-100158.limitations_and_considerations: The review notes several limitations including pro-innovation bias in literature, individual-blame bias, recall problems in diffusion research, and potential for innovations to widen socioeconomic gaps. MSD programs often lack detailed analysis of facilitation strategies and systematic application of behavior change science.
key_takeaways: MSD programs use five scale-up strategies: network-driven, firm-driven, policy improvement, quality standards, and supporting functions/BDS

More research is needed on stakeholder innovativeness, network structures, and strategic facilitation approaches

10568-100159.overview: A comprehensive learning series and toolkit designed to help coffee sector stakeholders understand, assess, and implement climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices to climate-proof their supply chains. The resource provides a structured 7-module approach covering climate risk assessment, adaptation strategies, scaling mechanisms, monitoring tools, and business case development for CSA investments.

resource_type.type_notes: Structured as a multi-module learning series with tools and frameworks embedded throughout

classification.development_stage_evidence: The series includes multiple case studies from Honduras, Uganda, Tanzania, Vietnam, and Brazil showing practical implementation and results

who_its_for.primary_audience: Coffee sector stakeholders including roasters, traders, producer organizations, development practitioners, and policymakers
who_its_for.organizational_context: Coffee companies, cooperatives, NGOs, development organizations, and government agencies working in coffee supply chains

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Coffee-producing regions facing climate variability and change

how_it_works.approach_summary: The approach follows a three-phase framework: Understand (climate risks and impacts), Design (appropriate CSA interventions), and Act (implement and scale solutions). It uses triangulation methods combining scientific data with farmer knowledge to develop context-specific adaptation plans.
key_steps: Understand climate change impacts on coffee production
Assess supply chain climate risks using risk profiles and vulnerability tools
Identify appropriate CSA practices, strategies, and enablers for context
Design scaling pathways including financing mechanisms
Implement monitoring and evaluation systems
Build business case for CSA investments
Establish collaborative action at origin
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 7-module series with 75-minute sessions, implementation timelines vary by intervention
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climate data, farm characteristics, socioeconomic data, soil analysis, yield data, and farmer surveys

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Access to climate data, mapping tools, survey platforms like SurveyCTO, weather stations
what_youll_need.human_resources: Extension agents, agronomists, data collectors, training facilitators
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with producer organizations, research institutions, and development agencies
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Varies by scale; examples include $300,000-$635,000 for multi-country cooperative support programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Climate risk profiles, vulnerability assessments, adaptation plans, trained extension agents, demonstration plots, and monitoring systems
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased adoption of climate-smart practices, improved farm resilience, enhanced supply chain sustainability, and reduced climate vulnerability
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Case studies show 25-100% yield increases, 30% water use reduction, and adoption rates of 36-100% for various practices after 2 years

practical_examples.description: Cover crops and temporary shade implementation in Ocotepeque region
practical_examples.key_result: Yield increased from 5.4 to 16.5 bags/hectare with income rising from $704 to $2,295

practical_examples.description: Water-saving irrigation technique in Central Highlands with 900+ farmers
practical_examples.key_result: 30% reduction in water use with no implementation costs

practical_examples.description: Conservation agriculture on maize within coffee systems with 200 farmers
practical_examples.key_result: 25-100% yield increases in first season and improved soil conservation

10568-100159.limitations_and_considerations: Challenges include farmer adoption barriers due to cost and risk, need for context-specific adaptation, requirement for long-term investment horizons that may not align with business cycles, and the complexity of measuring return on investment for climate adaptation.
key_takeaways: Climate change impacts on coffee are already occurring and require immediate action through tailored adaptation strategies
Successful CSA implementation requires understanding local climate risks, farmer capacity, and appropriate financing mechanisms
Scaling CSA requires multiple pathways including direct services, collaboration, and sector-level catalytic action
Effective monitoring combines farm-level indicators with landscape-level assessments to track resilience building
Business case development must connect climate risks to core business concerns like supply security, quality, and reputation

source_and_citation.full_citation: Selengia, Katherine; Sloan, Kealy; Glowka, Caroline; Lundy, Mark M.; Knueppel, Danielle; Teague, Elizabeth; Jassogne, Laurence T.P.; Giovannucci, Daniele; Ruíz, Pablo; Watene, George; Semroc, Bambi; Firl, Monica; Asten, Piet J.A. van; Taylor, Meredith (2018). Understand, Design, Act: Climate-proof your supply chain.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: Global Coffee Platform and Alliance for Resilient Coffee

10568-100161.overview: A comprehensive training manual for field officers to teach cocoa farmers climate-smart agriculture practices. The manual provides zone-specific recommendations across Ghana's three climate impact zones (coping/opportunity, adjustment, and transformation) with four adaptation levels (minimum, bronze, silver, gold) covering eight key topics from establishment to resilience building.

resource_type.type_notes: Explicitly described as a training manual with structured learning modules, trainer guidelines, and farmer fact sheets

classification.development_stage_evidence: The practices included in this manual have been drawn from the experience of cocoa farmers in each climate zone of Ghana, from experts and stakeholders in the Ghanaian cocoa sector

who_its_for.primary_audience: Field officers and extension agents training cocoa farmers
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural extension services, NGOs, development organizations working with cocoa farmers

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Cocoa-growing regions with varying climate change impacts

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses the SDF (Set up, Delivery, Finish) training model with zone-specific climate-smart agriculture recommendations. Trainers deliver structured sessions covering eight technical topics with hands-on demonstrations and exercises tailored to local climate impact zones.
key_steps: Assess local climate impact zone (coping/opportunity, adjustment, or transformation)
Determine farmer adaptation level (minimum, bronze, silver, gold)
Deliver topic-specific training using SDF model structure
Provide zone-appropriate recommendations for each agricultural practice
Conduct field demonstrations and practical exercises
Support farmers in implementing climate-smart practices
Monitor and adjust practices based on local conditions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Each topic session ranges from 45 minutes to 2 hours
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climate zone mapping, farm assessments, local agricultural conditions

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Training materials, flip charts, markers, demonstration plots, agricultural inputs for demonstrations
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained field officers/extension agents with agricultural and climate knowledge
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with agricultural extension services, access to farmer communities
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in detail, but requires investment in training materials and field demonstrations

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Trained field officers capable of delivering climate-smart agriculture training to cocoa farmers across different climate zones
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased farmer adoption of climate-smart practices leading to improved cocoa productivity and resilience to climate change
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Based on farmer experiences and expert knowledge from Ghana's cocoa sector, though specific impact metrics not provided

practical_examples.description: Zone-specific shade tree recommendations ranging from 15-25 trees per hectare in coping zones to 25-50 trees in transformation zones
practical_examples.key_result: Tailored adaptation strategies based on predicted climate impacts

practical_examples.description: Diversification strategies including cashew integration and alternative livelihood enterprises like beekeeping
practical_examples.key_result: Reduced dependency on cocoa as primary income source

10568-100161.limitations_and_considerations: Manual is specific to Ghana's context and climate zones. Requires significant trainer preparation and field demonstration capabilities. Some advanced practices like grafting require specialized skills and may not be suitable for all farmers. Availability of recommended inputs and planting materials may vary by location.
key_takeaways: Climate-smart agriculture requires zone-specific approaches based on predicted climate impacts
Four-level adaptation framework allows farmers to choose practices matching their capacity and willingness to invest
Comprehensive approach covers entire cocoa production cycle from establishment to post-harvest and resilience building
Integration of traditional knowledge with scientific recommendations enhances farmer adoption
Community-level approaches and landscape management are essential for effective climate adaptation

source_and_citation.full_citation: Dohmen, M.M., Noponen, M., Enomoto, R., Mensah, C., & Muilerman, S. (2018). Climate-Smart Agriculture in Cocoa: A Training Manual for Field Officers. World Cocoa Foundation and Rainforest Alliance.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: World Cocoa Foundation and Rainforest Alliance

10568-100168.overview: A comprehensive modeling platform developed by CIAT that uses agroclimatic, crop, livestock, and socio-economic models to help farmers and decision-makers make better agricultural decisions in the face of climate variability and change. The platform integrates seasonal climate forecasting with crop modeling to provide actionable information for agricultural planning and risk management.

resource_type.type_notes: Matches metadata - this is a modeling platform with multiple tools and services

classification.development_stage_evidence: Platform has been adopted by 2 farmer organizations in Colombia, covering 22 localities and potentially reaching 10,000 farmers, with 19 MTAs in Latin America involving around 200 institutions and 330,000 farmers

who_its_for.primary_audience: Farmers, agricultural decision-makers, researchers, and development practitioners
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural organizations, research institutions, government agencies, and farmer cooperatives

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Multiple crop systems including rice, beans, maize, banana, coffee, cassava, and livestock

how_it_works.approach_summary: Integrates agroclimatic modeling, crop and livestock modeling, and socio-economic modeling to create seasonal forecasts and long-term climate projections. Uses interdisciplinary research team to develop and implement modeling tools that predict climate, crop, and livestock behavior.
key_steps: Generate agroclimatic models using tools like CPT, R-CPT, and global/regional climate models
Apply crop and livestock models (ORYZA, DSSAT, GLAM, MANIHOT) with climate data
Integrate socio-economic modeling (IMPACT, Surplus, GTAP) to show economic scenarios
Provide seasonal forecasts and climate projections through platforms like AClimateColombia
Deliver information to farmers through various channels and platforms
Evaluate risks using participatory methods combined with crop and climate models
Support decision-making at farm, sub-national, national and regional levels
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Seasonal forecasts with long-term climate projections
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climate data (present and future), crop data, economic data, soil and agronomic management information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Access to climate models, crop modeling software (DSSAT, ORYZA, GLAM), statistical tools (CPT, R-CPT), and web platforms
what_youll_need.human_resources: Interdisciplinary team of researchers with expertise in climate science, crop modeling, and socio-economic analysis
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with agricultural organizations, research institutions, and government agencies
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in source document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Seasonal climate forecasts, crop yield predictions, economic scenario projections, and risk assessments delivered through web platforms and agricultural extension services
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved agricultural decision-making, reduced crop losses, enhanced climate resilience, and better adaptation to climate change
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Colombian rice farmers avoided losses exceeding US $3.6 million in 2014 following seasonal forecast advice; contributed to US $25 million investment in Kenya; reaching 330,000 farmers with climate and crop management information

practical_examples.description: Rice farmers used 10 years of connected climate and crop data with seasonal forecasting to make planting decisions
practical_examples.key_result: Avoided losses exceeding US $3.6 million in 2014

practical_examples.description: Climate risk assessments using participatory methods combined with crop and climate models
practical_examples.key_result: Contributed to US $25 million investment in climate-smart agriculture

practical_examples.description: Free agroclimatic services platform adopted by 2 farmer organizations covering 22 localities for maize and rice production
practical_examples.key_result: Potentially reaching 10,000 farmers across 8 departments

10568-100168.limitations_and_considerations: No limitations explicitly discussed in the source document.
key_takeaways: Integrates multiple modeling approaches (climate, crop, livestock, socio-economic) to provide comprehensive agricultural decision support
Demonstrates significant economic impact with documented avoided losses and leveraged investments
Operates at multiple scales from individual farms to regional level across Latin America, Asia, and Africa
Combines cutting-edge modeling science with practical delivery through web platforms and extension services
Has received multiple UN awards recognizing innovative use of Big Data and seasonal forecasting for agricultural applications

source_and_citation.full_citation: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (2019). Modelos de Predicción para Mejores Decisiones. CIAT.

10568-100171.overview: This resource describes climate information services that deliver site-specific, reliable climate information to farmers to enable better informed farming decisions. It focuses on Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs) and Participatory Climate Services (PICSA) as innovative approaches to help farmers prepare for climate variability and reduce crop losses.

resource_type.type_notes: Content describes ongoing climate services programs and their implementation across multiple countries, with outcomes and impact data

classification.development_stage_evidence: Programs operating across 4 Latin American countries and 1 African country, with established outcomes reaching 330K farmers in Latin America and 1 million farmers in Rwanda

who_its_for.primary_audience: Farmers, farmer organizations, agricultural technicians, and national meteorological services
who_its_for.organizational_context: Farmer organizations, national meteorological services, climate outlook forums

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Areas where climate is predictable and crops are highly sensitive to climate

how_it_works.approach_summary: Climate services generate, translate, transfer, and use climate knowledge to provide site-specific, reliable climate information to farmers in a demand-driven and timely manner. Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees facilitate open dialogues about seasonal climate forecasts and help devise measures to reduce crop losses.
key_steps: Establish Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs)
Generate seasonal climate forecasts
Translate climate information for agricultural context
Facilitate dialogues between stakeholders about climate variations
Provide planting recommendations and variety selection guidance
Deliver timely, site-specific information to farmers
Monitor and evaluate outcomes
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Seasonal climate forecasting data, agricultural production data, local meteorological information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Agro-climatic prediction platform, seasonal climate forecasting capabilities, big data analytics
what_youll_need.human_resources: Climate scientists, agricultural technicians, local stakeholders, meteorologists
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships between farmer organizations, national meteorological services, and climate outlook forums

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Site-specific climate information, planting recommendations, variety selection guidance, seasonal forecasts
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Better informed farming decisions, reduced crop losses, improved resilience to climate variability, increased productivity
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Colombian rice farmers avoided US $3.6 million in losses in 2014; some bean farmers in Colombia are doubling and sometimes tripling productivity; 330K farmers in Latin America making better decisions using agro-climatic information

practical_examples.description: Agro-climatic prediction platform provides recommendations to farmers about when to plant and what varieties to plant for rice and maize across 22 localities in 8 departments
practical_examples.key_result: Some bean farmers are doubling and sometimes tripling productivity

practical_examples.description: Analysis of 10-year data helped rice farmers avoid significant crop losses through better climate information
practical_examples.key_result: Avoided losses of US $3.6 million for Colombian rice farmers in 2014

practical_examples.description: Climate information services implemented across 30 districts to help farmers prepare for climate variability
practical_examples.key_result: 1 million farmers receiving climate information and better prepared to face climate variability

10568-100171.limitations_and_considerations: Climate services have the most potential where climate is predictable and the crops farmers grow are highly sensitive to climate. According to FAO, 70% of food comes from small-scale farmers, most of whom have no access to climate information.
key_takeaways: Local Technical Agro-climatic Committees (LTACs) provide innovative platforms for stakeholders to access and discuss seasonal climate forecasts
Site-specific, demand-driven climate information enables better farming decisions and can significantly reduce crop losses
Climate services work best in areas where climate is predictable and crops are highly climate-sensitive
Successful implementation requires partnerships between farmer organizations, meteorological services, and climate outlook forums
Evidence shows substantial economic benefits, with documented cases of avoided losses and increased productivity

source_and_citation.full_citation: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (2019). Putting Climate Information in the Hands of Farmers.

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Some formatting artifacts and geographic location text scattered throughout, but core content is clear and comprehensive

10568-100172.overview: This is a NAMA (Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Action) proposal framework for sustainable cattle production in Colombia, designed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from livestock while promoting sustainable productive landscapes. The proposal outlines a 9-month implementation strategy to develop mitigation actions, establish baselines, and create monitoring systems for the livestock sector.

resource_type.type_notes: Matches metadata - this is a structured proposal framework for implementing NAMAs in cattle production

classification.development_stage_evidence: The document states 'The consolidation of this project will make it possible to define the need for financial support, technology transfer and capacity building to continue with the implementation of mitigation actions'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Government agencies, livestock federations, and development organizations implementing climate mitigation strategies
who_its_for.organizational_context: National governments, livestock associations, research centers, and international development organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Livestock production systems in tropical and subtropical regions

how_it_works.approach_summary: The NAMA framework develops regional-level strategies to reduce GHG emissions from livestock production through improved farm management, enhanced carbon sinks in agro-ecosystems, and conservation of natural ecosystems. It involves establishing baselines, designing monitoring systems, and creating implementation plans.
key_steps: Select NAMA coordinator and describe sector importance
Analyze barriers and establish national/departmental GHG emissions baseline
Develop mitigation scenarios and prioritize regions for implementation
Calculate baseline emissions for slaughterhouses and livestock auctions
Create financial model and measurement, reporting, verification system
Develop implementation plan with logistics evaluation
Design scaling strategy for prioritized geographical regions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 9 months execution period
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: GHG emissions data, livestock production statistics, slaughterhouse and auction data, regional agricultural information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: GHG measurement tools, SIDESS tool for ruminant modeling, traceability systems
what_youll_need.human_resources: NAMA coordinator, technical specialists from multiple organizations (FEDEGAN, CIPAV, WRI, CIAT)
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Coordination with Ministry of Environment, Ministry of Agriculture, DNP, and Sustainable Colombian Livestock Project
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: International funding support required, specific amounts not detailed

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Four key documents: mitigation strategy, baseline and GHG scenarios, financial component, and implementation plan
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: 20% reduction in GHG emissions by 2030, increased carbon sinks in agro-ecosystems, conservation of natural ecosystems
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: NAMA was registered in NAMA Registry in November 2015 and prioritized in agricultural portfolio for NDC implementation

practical_examples.description: NAMA registered in NAMA Registry in November 2015 as voluntary commitment before COP 21
practical_examples.key_result: Prioritized in agricultural portfolio as vehicle for meeting 20% GHG reduction goal by 2030

10568-100172.limitations_and_considerations: Implementation depends on securing international financial support, technology transfer, and capacity building. Requires coordination across multiple government ministries and organizations. Success contingent on effective monitoring, reporting, and verification systems.
key_takeaways: Provides structured approach to developing NAMAs for livestock sector climate mitigation
Integrates multiple stakeholders including government, private sector, and research organizations
Emphasizes regional-level implementation with national coordination
Combines emissions reduction with ecosystem conservation and sustainable production
Requires comprehensive baseline establishment and monitoring systems for effectiveness

10568-100177.overview: The CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Activity Report documents the development and piloting of an ICT-based CSA Calculator tool for farm-level monitoring across CCAFS Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The tool quantitatively assesses climate-smartness of farms by linking indicators from the three CSA pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) with farm resource analysis.

resource_type.type_notes: This is an activity report documenting the development and implementation of a monitoring tool, not the tool itself

classification.development_stage_evidence: Data were collected in 7 of the 9 countries where the CSA calculator has been calibrated (Ghana, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Uganda, Nepal and Bangladesh)

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders implementing climate-smart agriculture monitoring
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, development organizations, agricultural extension services

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Diverse farming systems including coffee, sugar cane, maize, cassava, rice production with mixed crop-livestock systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: The CSA calculator uses a whole-farm modeling approach to assess trade-offs and synergies between CSA pillars through quantitative indicators. It was adapted from an Excel-based tool to an ICT application integrated into the CSV monitoring system.
key_steps: Calibrate tool parameters for specific country contexts and farming systems
Train supervisors and local enumerators on data collection methods
Collect farm-level data through structured surveys covering family size, CSA practices, crop areas, livestock, inputs, and prices
Calculate indicators for productivity (caloric ratio, fodder ratio, cost-benefit ratio), adaptation (biodiversity index, water balance, nutrient balance), and mitigation (CO2 emissions/sequestration)
Analyze results using typology analysis to identify different adoption patterns
Compare climate-smartness across different farm types and CSA practice portfolios
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Implementation period: January – December 2018
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Family size, CSA practices currently tested, areas of main crops, number of animals, amount and prices of inputs used, sale prices, management of crop residues

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: ICT-based application, CoolFarm Tool (version 2.0 Beta 3) for emissions calculations
what_youll_need.human_resources: 14 Supervisors and 42 local enumerators were trained; enumerators preferably with bachelor degree in agronomy or economy for unit conversions
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Collaboration with regional teams and flagship projects across CSV sites
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Quantitative assessment of farm-level climate-smartness across three CSA pillars, typology of CSA adoption patterns, cross-site comparison of farming systems
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Strengthened quantitative assessment of CSA at farm level in CSV monitoring, better understanding of CSA practice effectiveness and adoption patterns
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully collected data from 263 farmers across 7 countries, identified three distinct adoption types in Ghana analysis

practical_examples.description: Analysis of 191 households identified three adoption types: 'adopters' (mainly men with higher productive area), 'non-adopters' (mixed gender with low productive area), and 'poor-adopters' (higher proportion of women, affected by climate, having access to loans).
practical_examples.key_result: Farm representative of adopter type 1 showed best performances across all three CSA pillars compared to poor-adopter type 3, despite implementing similar practice portfolios

practical_examples.description: Initial development and testing of the CSA calculator tool using Excel-based format before adaptation to other countries.
practical_examples.key_result: First version successfully developed and tested, providing foundation for multi-country adaptation

10568-100177.limitations_and_considerations: Going from Excel tool to ICT application made data entry more rigid, particularly regarding units used for crops and inputs. Main challenge was identifying enumerators with sufficient technical background for unit conversions. Complex farming systems (like in Vietnam) may require focusing on main crops representing 50-70% of cropping areas rather than all farm components.
key_takeaways: Whole-farm modeling approach enables assessment of synergies and trade-offs between CSA pillars at farm level
Tool successfully adapted across 9 countries with diverse farming systems, though complexity varies significantly between sites
Integration into ICT-based monitoring system requires careful attention to data standardization and enumerator training
Typology analysis reveals distinct adoption patterns linked to socio-economic characteristics and farm constraints
Land tenure arrangements significantly influence potential for land use changes and CSA implementation

source_and_citation.full_citation: Andrieu, Nadine; Bonilla Findji, Osana (2019). Activity Report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation. CGIAR.

10568-100180.overview: The Rainforest Alliance Sustainable Agriculture Standard for Smallholder Farms is a comprehensive certification framework designed to maximize positive social, environmental, and economic impact in agricultural production. It provides smallholder farmers with an enhanced framework to improve their livelihoods while protecting the landscapes where they live and work through a performance-driven approach that emphasizes continuous improvement.

resource_type.type_notes: Clearly identified as a sustainable agriculture standard with detailed criteria and requirements

classification.development_stage_evidence: This is a draft standard V1.0 for public consultation (November 2018) that harmonizes existing standards of the Rainforest Alliance and UTZ, indicating it builds on field-tested approaches

who_its_for.primary_audience: Smallholder farmers and producer groups in tree crops, fruits, nuts, and cut flowers
who_its_for.organizational_context: Producer groups, cooperatives, associations, and supply chain actors managing smallholder certification

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Global scope focusing on tree crops (coffee, cocoa, tea), fruits (bananas, coconuts, pineapples), nuts (hazelnuts), and cut flowers

how_it_works.approach_summary: The standard uses a performance-driven approach with core criteria for initial certification and improvement pathways for continuous progress. It combines prescriptive practices with outcome measurement through data collection and indicators.
key_steps: Conduct risk assessment using Rainforest Alliance tools
Implement core criteria across management, farming practices, social, and environmental areas
Establish internal management system and member administration
Commit to minimum 12 improvement topics (mandatory, context-specific, and self-selected)
Collect and report data through indicators for monitoring progress
Undergo certification audit and continuous improvement cycles
Maintain traceability and premium transparency systems
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Annual internal inspections, certification audits every few years with continuous improvement cycles
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Member registry data, yield records, GPS coordinates, wage records, training attendance, agrochemical application records, and sustainability indicators

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: GPS mapping capabilities, data collection systems, internal inspection protocols, and access to Rainforest Alliance assessment tools
what_youll_need.human_resources: Group management with adequate capacities, trained internal inspectors, agrochemical handlers with proper training, and community-based monitoring personnel
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Organized producer group structure (association, cooperative, or managed entity), internal management system, and grievance mechanisms
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Certified sustainable agricultural production, improved farm management systems, enhanced traceability, and documented continuous improvement
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved farmer livelihoods, enhanced environmental protection, better working conditions, increased productivity and profitability, and progress toward living income
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: The standard builds on existing Rainforest Alliance and UTZ programs with positive market feedback, though specific effectiveness data not provided in this draft

10568-100180.limitations_and_considerations: The standard requires significant management capacity and resources for implementation. It may be challenging for very small or poorly organized groups. The improvement pathway approach requires sustained commitment and investment. Some criteria may conflict with local practices or economic constraints.
key_takeaways: Performance-driven approach combines core compliance criteria with flexible improvement pathways for continuous progress
Comprehensive coverage includes management, farming practices, social conditions, and environmental protection
Strong emphasis on data collection and indicator-based monitoring for evidence-based improvement
Differentiated requirements for smallholders versus medium/large producers to ensure relevance
Focus on assess-and-address approaches for sensitive issues like child labor, forced labor, and harassment rather than simple prohibition

10568-100181.overview: This document summarizes how the 2017 Sustainable Agriculture Network (SAN) Standard integrates climate-smart agriculture principles into its certification framework. It explains how the standard addresses climate change adaptation and mitigation in agricultural systems through three main principles: effective planning and management, biodiversity conservation, and natural resource conservation.

resource_type.type_notes: Aligns with metadata - describes a certification standard framework

classification.development_stage_evidence: The document describes the 2017 SAN Standard as an updated version of an existing certification system, indicating it is actively implemented

who_its_for.primary_audience: Farmers, agricultural producers, and certification bodies
who_its_for.organizational_context: Agricultural producers, certification organizations, supply chain companies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical and subtropical agricultural systems, particularly shade-tolerant crops like coffee and cocoa

how_it_works.approach_summary: The framework integrates climate-smart agriculture into three main principles: effective planning and management (including climate vulnerability assessments), biodiversity conservation (protecting native ecosystems), and natural resource conservation (improving soil health and reducing chemical inputs).
key_steps: Conduct ongoing climate vulnerability assessments
Develop action plans to address climate threats
Implement biodiversity conservation measures including native shade trees
Apply integrated pest management to reduce chemical pesticide use
Maintain or improve soil health and water management
Reduce energy and fossil fuel consumption
Protect existing forests and avoid deforestation
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climate vulnerability assessments, farm management plans, biodiversity inventories

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Capacity for climate vulnerability assessments, integrated pest management knowledge, soil and water management techniques
what_youll_need.human_resources: Group administrators for smallholders, technical assistance providers, consultants for assessments
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Certification body oversight, training programs, local guidance materials

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Climate-adaptive farm management plans, reduced chemical pesticide use, enhanced biodiversity conservation, improved soil health
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased farm resilience to climate change, reduced greenhouse gas emissions, enhanced productivity and incomes, landscape-level environmental benefits
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Qualitative evidence that climate smart agriculture helps individual farms achieve greater climate adaptation and resilience

practical_examples.description: Smallholder cocoa farmers facing drought and heat focus on enhancing soil moisture, reducing solar radiation, and creating better microclimates
practical_examples.key_result: Enhanced climate resilience for cocoa production

practical_examples.description: Indigenous community affected by heavy rain and flooding implements trenching, cover crops, and erosion management
practical_examples.key_result: Better soil properties and flood management after harvest losses in 2014

10568-100181.limitations_and_considerations: Climate conditions and impacts are highly variable depending on region, farm, and crop. The quality of prior climate vulnerability assessments and availability of information varies significantly between regions, with Latin America having more available information than other regions.
key_takeaways: The 2017 SAN Standard integrates climate-smart agriculture into its core principles rather than treating it as an optional add-on

practical_examples.description: Women practice poultry sharing between male-headed and female-headed households as a traditional mechanism for accessing livestock assets
practical_examples.key_result: Provides entry point for helping rural women step out of poverty through livestock ladder approach

practical_examples.description: Men take control of income from milk sales traditionally controlled by women due to institutional requirements for household head registration
practical_examples.key_result: Creates intrahousehold conflicts and challenges women's traditional control over dairy income

10568-100208.limitations_and_considerations: Existing studies are scanty and localized in geographic coverage and gender issues covered. Most research uses generalized approaches to livestock ownership that may mask individual or joint ownership patterns. Limited understanding of local meanings of gender concepts like 'access' and 'control' across different contexts. Research approaches often overlook women's roles, reinforcing men's visibility as pioneers of livelihood diversification.
key_takeaways: Women play major roles in livestock husbandry and management but their contributions are often culturally undervalued or invisible
Men control political aspects of livestock while women handle labor-intensive reproductive activities across all livestock species
Women face greater constraints in accessing, owning, and controlling livestock resources compared to men
Commercialization of livestock products often shifts control from women to men, creating intrahousehold tensions
Gender capacity development among livestock practitioners is essential for effective gender mainstreaming in interventions

10568-100216.overview: This is a comprehensive situation analysis report examining the intersection of climate change, food security, and nutrition in Uganda, with detailed case studies from Mbale and Nwoya districts. The report analyzes policy frameworks, institutional arrangements, and community-level experiences to identify gaps and opportunities for implementing gender- and nutrition-sensitive climate smart agriculture interventions.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive research report documenting situation analysis findings with policy analysis, field research, and recommendations

classification.development_stage_evidence: The report documents extensive field research including 32 expert interviews and 25 focus group discussions conducted across two districts

who_its_for.primary_audience: Government policymakers, development practitioners, researchers working on climate-agriculture-nutrition nexus
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, international development organizations, research institutions, NGOs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Mixed farming systems in highland and lowland areas with bimodal and unimodal rainfall patterns

how_it_works.approach_summary: The analysis employed a four-step qualitative approach combining desk research, expert interviews, focus group discussions, and policy analysis to understand climate risks, institutional responses, and community experiences.
key_steps: Extensive literature and policy document review (50 documents)
Expert interviews with 32 government and non-government actors
Focus group discussions with 25 community groups (women, men, youth)
Policy analysis using 10 criteria framework for gender and nutrition sensitivity
Data analysis and validation through stakeholder workshops
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Data collection conducted from June to October 2018
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Policy documents, expert interviews, community focus groups, climate data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Qualitative data analysis software (NVIVO), policy analysis framework
what_youll_need.human_resources: Research team with expertise in climate change, nutrition, gender analysis, and policy assessment
