expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Report identifies specific policy gaps and provides actionable recommendations for multi-sectoral coordination and implementation

practical_examples.description: Highland district experiencing landslides, flooding, and prolonged dry spells affecting crop production and household food security
practical_examples.key_result: Identified specific adaptation strategies like contour trenching and diversification, but noted lack of landscape-level coordination

practical_examples.description: Northern district with unimodal rainfall facing extreme temperatures, pest infestations, and human-wildlife conflict affecting agriculture
practical_examples.key_result: Documented community coping strategies including crop rotation, small-scale irrigation, and livelihood diversification

10568-100216.limitations_and_considerations: Analysis limited to qualitative methods without household-level quantitative data on nutrition indicators; some national nutrition programs lack documented frameworks; late implementation in Ethiopia meant only Uganda findings are reported; policy document review may not be exhaustive given cross-cutting nature of issues.

Climate risks disproportionately affect women, youth, and vulnerable populations who have limited access to adaptation technologies and resources
Multi-sectoral coordination platforms are needed to minimize duplication and enhance knowledge sharing among diverse actors
Landscape-level approaches are essential for effective climate smart agriculture adoption rather than individual farmer targeting
Legal frameworks for climate change mainstreaming (similar to gender and equity budgeting requirements) could improve priority and resource allocation

10568-100217.overview: This is a midline synthesis report documenting changes in agricultural practices, food security, and climate adaptation in Ghana's Lawra-Jirapa region seven years after CCAFS baseline studies. The report assesses whether households and communities are successfully adapting to climate change through improved farming practices, diversification strategies, and enhanced access to information and resources.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive synthesis report of midline survey findings comparing 2011 baseline to 2018 data

classification.development_stage_evidence: Seven years after the implementation of the baseline studies, the CCAFS program carried out surveys again in Lawra-Jirapa, Ghana as a test case to help assess whether a midline assessment in other sites is worth the substantial investment

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate adaptation and food security
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, development organizations, government agencies, NGOs working in climate-smart agriculture

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Semi-arid savanna region with unreliable rainfall and poor soil fertility

how_it_works.approach_summary: The midline assessment revisited the same 140 households from the 2011 baseline survey using standardized tools to measure changes in farming practices, food security, asset ownership, and climate adaptation strategies over a seven-year period.
key_steps: Conduct household surveys using quantitative questionnaires
Implement village baseline studies using participatory approaches
Carry out organizational surveys with key community organizations
Compare midline data with baseline indicators
Analyze changes in adaptation, mitigation, and food security indices
Organize community feedback workshops to validate findings
Synthesize results across household, village, and organizational levels
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Seven-year interval between baseline (2011) and midline (2018) assessments
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Household demographic data, farming practices, asset ownership, food security indicators, organizational membership, information access patterns

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Standardized survey instruments, data collection tools, statistical analysis capabilities

what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with local research institutions, community engagement protocols, ethical clearance
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Substantial investment as noted in the document, requiring multi-year commitment

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive data on changes in farming practices, food security status, asset ownership, organizational membership, and information access patterns
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced understanding of climate adaptation effectiveness and improved targeting of future interventions
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Food security improved with 36% of households experiencing 5+ hunger months compared to 47% in 2011; 49% of households now in high asset ownership category; increased use of improved seeds from 11% to 50%

practical_examples.description: Seven-year tracking of 140 households showed significant improvements in education levels, asset ownership, and food security despite reduced diversification.
practical_examples.key_result: Households experiencing severe food insecurity (5+ hunger months) decreased from 47% to 36%, while high-level asset ownership increased from 33% to 49%

10568-100217.limitations_and_considerations: The study was conducted as a test case in only one site, limiting generalizability. Reduced adaptation and mitigation practices may indicate technology lock-in rather than improved resilience. Some positive changes may be attributed to external factors like government rural electrification rather than CCAFS interventions specifically.
key_takeaways: Food security and asset ownership improved significantly over the seven-year period, suggesting positive adaptation outcomes
Households shifted from high diversification to more specialized production, potentially increasing market-oriented income but reducing risk management
Climate and weather remain the primary drivers of agricultural changes, indicating continued vulnerability
Access to information improved substantially through mobile phones and radio, particularly benefiting women
Midline assessments provide valuable insights for tracking long-term adaptation effectiveness and can inform future intervention design

10568-100222.overview: This document provides technical guidelines for incorporating silvopastoral systems (SSP) into national greenhouse gas inventory measurement, reporting, and verification (MRV) systems under the UNFCCC framework. It addresses the challenge that while silvopastoral systems have recognized climate mitigation potential in Latin America, they remain invisible in national climate accounting due to lack of proper MRV frameworks.

resource_type.type_notes: Explicitly labeled as guidelines and provides technical orientations and a roadmap for MRV implementation

classification.development_stage_evidence: The document states that 'ningún país ha reportado FE con validez nacional específicos para estos sistemas' (no country has reported nationally valid emission factors for these systems)

who_its_for.primary_audience: Climate change mitigation focal points, environment and agriculture ministry representatives, and UNFCCC national inventory experts
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies responsible for national greenhouse gas inventories and climate policy implementation

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical and subtropical livestock production systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: The guidelines provide a structured approach using decision trees to help countries determine how to classify silvopastoral systems within IPCC land use categories and develop appropriate measurement and reporting methodologies for national greenhouse gas inventories.
key_steps: Establish national definitions of silvopastoral systems aligned with climate mitigation objectives
Determine parameters and ranges to distinguish between different SSP typologies
Generate methodological guide for attributing SSP to IPCC land use categories
Develop activity data through national statistics or spatial information
Generate country-specific emission factors
Integrate SSP MRV into national inventory systems
Report progress through national communications and biennial update reports
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Short to medium-term roadmap implementation
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Land use statistics, spatial data, carbon content measurements, tree density data, forage quality analyses

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: GIS systems, remote sensing capabilities, field measurement protocols for carbon content and biomass
what_youll_need.human_resources: Technical experts in greenhouse gas inventories, forestry specialists, livestock experts, institutional coordination capacity
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Coordination between environment and agriculture ministries, statistical agencies, and research institutions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but notes significant financial, technological and institutional requirements

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: National definitions of silvopastoral systems, activity data and emission factors, integration into national greenhouse gas inventories
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Making silvopastoral systems visible in national climate accounting, supporting evidence-based policy decisions, enabling access to climate finance
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Colombia has successfully included intensive silvopastoral systems in its national inventory using IPCC Tier 1 methods

practical_examples.description: Successfully included intensive silvopastoral systems in national greenhouse gas inventory using national statistics
practical_examples.key_result: First country in region to report SSP emissions and removals in national inventory

practical_examples.description: Designing national monitoring system (SIMOCUTE) to monitor all land uses including silvopastoral systems
practical_examples.key_result: Transitioning from forest-only monitoring to comprehensive land use monitoring

practical_examples.description: NAMA Ganadería includes silvopastoral systems with estimated mitigation potential of 4 MtCO2e by 2030
practical_examples.key_result: Quantified mitigation targets for livestock sector transformation

10568-100222.limitations_and_considerations: Multiple challenges including heterogeneous country readiness levels, lack of standardized definitions, limited technical and financial capacity, difficulties in remote sensing detection of agroforestry systems, and potential conflicts with bilateral deforestation agreements when reclassifying land uses.
key_takeaways: Silvopastoral systems have significant climate mitigation potential but remain invisible in national climate accounting across Latin America
Establishing clear national definitions aligned with IPCC land use categories is the critical first step for MRV implementation
Countries need coordinated institutional frameworks between environment and agriculture sectors to successfully implement SSP monitoring
The approach should start with broad SSP categories before advancing to specific typologies based on country priorities and capacities
Regional cooperation through networks like RedINGEI can facilitate knowledge transfer and capacity building for MRV implementation

CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).

10568-100233.overview: GeoFarmer is a digital monitoring and evaluation tool developed for Climate-Smart Villages (CSVs) that enables efficient data collection through smartphone applications and web dashboards. This ICT-based solution was designed to monitor climate-smart agriculture practices across CCAFS sites, providing near real-time data flows to support agricultural development projects and decision-making by farmers and policymakers.

resource_type.type_notes: The document describes GeoFarmer as an ICT tool with specific functionalities, mobile app, and web dashboard components

classification.development_stage_evidence: Since 2017, we implemented the monitoring plan in nine CCAFS sites... collected about 300,000 data records

who_its_for.primary_audience: Agricultural development practitioners, researchers, and local enumerators working in Climate-Smart Villages
who_its_for.organizational_context: CGIAR centers, agricultural research organizations, development projects implementing climate-smart agriculture

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Rural agricultural areas with limited mobile internet coverage

how_it_works.approach_summary: GeoFarmer uses logical survey trees with connectors between questions, allowing facilitators to conduct interviews with farmers using smartphones. Data is collected offline and synchronized when internet is available, then analyzed using R scripts to calculate predefined indicators.
key_steps: Register farmers with electronic consent
Design logical survey trees with conditional connectors
Train local facilitators and enumerators
Conduct interviews using smartphone app
Synchronize data to cloud server when internet available
Analyze data using R scripts to calculate indicators
Generate results and upload to dashboard
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: One week training per site, ongoing data collection
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Farmer demographic data, climate events, livelihood security, food security, CSA adoption information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Smartphones, internet connectivity for data synchronization, cloud server access
what_youll_need.human_resources: Regional coordinators, local enumerators/facilitators, research partners, trainers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with local research organizations and extension services
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: One week training per site, ongoing facilitator time for data collection

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Standardized data collection on farm performance (7 indicators), household effects (20 indicators), and adoption trends (17 indicators)
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Better decision-making by farmers and policymakers through near real-time monitoring of climate-smart agriculture practices
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully collected about 300,000 data records across nine CCAFS sites with over 3,000 farmers

practical_examples.description: Implemented monitoring with 357 farmers (189 men, 168 women)
practical_examples.key_result: Collected 66,841 data records

practical_examples.description: Largest implementation with 458 farmers (229 men, 229 women)
practical_examples.key_result: Collected 53,177 data records

practical_examples.description: Initial pilot with 300 farmers (123 men, 131 women)
practical_examples.key_result: Collected 19,869 data records

10568-100233.limitations_and_considerations: Mobile internet coverage is rarely available in rural areas where CSVs are located, requiring offline functionality. Enumerators must fully understand the meaning of each question to translate appropriately to local language and cultural context. Data synchronization functionality is essential but was not available in early pilots.
key_takeaways: ICT-based monitoring tools are more cost-effective and suitable for large-scale agricultural development projects than traditional methods
Logical survey trees with conditional connectors (5Q approach) make surveys more effective and avoid redundant questions
Offline functionality with smart synchronization is crucial for rural areas with limited internet connectivity
Comprehensive training of local enumerators is essential for proper question translation and cultural adaptation
The tool successfully scaled across multiple regions and collected substantial amounts of monitoring data

source_and_citation.full_citation: Eitzinger, A., Bonilla Findji, O., Andrieu, N., Feil, C., & Bejarano, G. (2019). Activity report: CSV Monitoring and Evaluation Plan. CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

10568-100240.overview: This is a Master's dissertation that evaluates the economic effectiveness of newly introduced AVRDC (Asian Vegetable Research Development Centre) vegetable production technologies compared to traditional farming practices in Babati District, Tanzania, specifically examining their impact on income poverty reduction among smallholder farmers.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a Master's dissertation submitted to Sokoine University of Agriculture, despite metadata labeling it as Training Manual/Framework

classification.development_stage_evidence: The study assessed technologies that were already introduced and being used by farmers: 'AVRDC has introduced new technologies to farmers in Babati District but, the introduction of technologies is not enough unless they are economically effective'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Agricultural researchers, development practitioners, policymakers, and AVRDC stakeholders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, NGOs working in vegetable production

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Semi-arid regions suitable for vegetable production with irrigation potential

how_it_works.approach_summary: The study used a panel research design with paired sample T-tests to compare the economic performance of 77 farmers before and after AVRDC technology intervention, measuring yield, costs, revenues, and farmer perceptions of vegetable quality.
key_steps: Baseline data collection on traditional farming practices
Introduction of AVRDC technologies including improved seeds, pest management, fertilizer application, and water management
Training farmers on good agricultural practices
Follow-up survey to measure outcomes
Statistical analysis using paired sample T-tests
Assessment of farmer perceptions on vegetable quality
Cost-benefit analysis comparing traditional vs. new technologies
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Study covered crop seasons from 2013-2014
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Baseline farmer data, production costs, yields, revenues, farmer perception surveys

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Statistical analysis software for paired sample T-tests, survey instruments
what_youll_need.human_resources: Agricultural economists, extension workers, trained enumerators for data collection

what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comparative analysis of yield, costs, revenues, and farmer perceptions between traditional and improved farming practices
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Income poverty reduction through adoption of cost-effective vegetable production technologies
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Study found significant differences: mean yield increased from 3,122.5 kg to 4,279.2 kg (p=0.006), and net profit increased from 746,430 TShs to 1,031,610 TShs despite higher production costs

practical_examples.description: AVRDC introduced improved vegetable technologies to farmers in four villages (Matufa, Seloto, Bermi, and Galapo) focusing on tomatoes, African eggplant, amaranth, and sweet pepper production.
practical_examples.key_result: Farmers achieved 1,156.7 kg more yield per acre and significantly higher net profits despite reduced cultivation area

10568-100240.limitations_and_considerations: Study faced challenges with respondent attrition (some farmers died or moved), limited to specific geographic area, and focused primarily on short-term economic impacts rather than long-term sustainability or environmental effects.
key_takeaways: AVRDC technologies significantly increased vegetable yields and farmer profits compared to traditional practices
Despite higher production costs, the new technologies generated substantially higher net revenues
94% of farmers had favorable perceptions of vegetable quality produced under new technologies
Technologies proved cost-effective for income poverty reduction among smallholder vegetable farmers
Success depends on technology affordability and accessibility to farmers with limited resources

10568-100245.overview: This is a PhD thesis that presents a comprehensive whole genome-based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia, analyzing genetic diversity, population structure, and selection signatures using advanced genomic techniques including whole genome sequencing and microsatellite analysis.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a doctoral dissertation/thesis, which is a form of academic paper

classification.development_stage_evidence: This study aims to characterize the genomic diversity of indigenous chicken populations from Ethiopia... understanding the level of genetic diversity at genome level within and among chicken populations is of paramount importance in identifying potential chicken populations for genetic improvement programs

who_its_for.primary_audience: Scientists, researchers, and geneticists working on livestock breeding and conservation
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural research centers, universities, and breeding programs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Multiple agro-ecological zones across Ethiopia including tepid to cool moist mid highlands, hot to warm humid lowlands, and various elevation ranges from 1800-2400 masl

how_it_works.approach_summary: The study employs whole genome sequencing and microsatellite analysis to characterize genetic diversity, population structure, and selection signatures in Ethiopian indigenous chicken populations across different agro-ecological zones.
key_steps: Whole blood sample collection from 260+ birds across 27 indigenous chicken populations
DNA isolation and quality checking
Library preparation and whole genome sequencing
Variant calling and SNP discovery (21+ million SNPs identified)
Population structure analysis using PCA and admixture analysis
Selective sweep detection using pooled heterozygosity and Fst methods
Functional annotation and gene ontology analysis
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-year PhD research project completed in 2018
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Whole blood samples from indigenous chickens, genomic DNA, reference genome sequences, bioinformatics tools and pipelines

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Molecular laboratory facilities, DNA sequencing platforms, bioinformatics software (BWA, GATK, Picard), computational resources for genome analysis
what_youll_need.human_resources: Molecular biologists, bioinformaticians, field sampling teams, laboratory technicians
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Research institutions with genomics capabilities, partnerships with agricultural research centers
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Significant funding for genomic sequencing, laboratory equipment, and computational infrastructure

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive genomic characterization including 21+ million SNPs (72% novel), population structure analysis revealing four ancestral gene pools, identification of 595 candidate genes under selection
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced understanding of genetic diversity for chicken breeding programs, identification of genomic regions for trait improvement, conservation of indigenous genetic resources
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully identified significant genomic diversity with most variants previously undescribed in commercial breeds, revealed selection signatures associated with production and reproduction traits

practical_examples.description: Selective breeding program showed substantial improvements in indigenous chicken performance under scavenging conditions
practical_examples.key_result: Survival improved from <50% to 98% in generation 6, body weight increased from 550g to 880g at 16 weeks, egg production tripled from 24 to 72 per annum

10568-100245.limitations_and_considerations: The study focuses specifically on Ethiopian populations which may limit generalizability to other African contexts. Requires significant technical expertise and resources for implementation. Some genomic regions showed lower SNP density particularly on sex chromosomes.
key_takeaways: Ethiopian indigenous chickens possess significant genomic diversity with over 21 million SNPs identified, 72% of which were novel
Four distinct ancestral gene pools exist across Ethiopian chicken populations based on genomic analysis
595 candidate genes under positive selection were identified, related to production and reproduction traits
Indigenous populations show remarkable genetic potential for improvement while maintaining adaptation to local conditions
Whole genome approaches provide superior resolution for understanding genetic diversity compared to traditional microsatellite methods

source_and_citation.full_citation: Belew, A.K. (2018). Whole genome based characterization of indigenous chicken populations in Ethiopia. PhD Thesis, Addis Ababa University.

10568-100247.overview: This is a master's thesis that develops a spatial mapping approach to identify feed constraints and system characteristics in smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa. It creates 16 domains based on feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability, and water availability to predict suitable climate-resilient feed interventions using the FEAST/Techfit logic.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a master's thesis submitted to NUI Galway, though metadata labels it as 'Tool'

classification.development_stage_evidence: This study focused on two key feed constraints and two adoption factors in the mixed farming system of East Africa... Four maps showing feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability and water availability were produced using geographic information system (GIS)

who_its_for.primary_audience: Researchers, development practitioners, and policymakers working on livestock feed systems
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, development organizations, agricultural extension services

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Smallholder mixed farming systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses GIS mapping to overlay four variables (feed quantity, seasonal scarcity, land availability, water availability) to create 16 spatial domains, then applies FEAST/Techfit logic to predict suitable feed interventions for each domain.
key_steps: Map feed quantity using dry matter productivity and cattle density data
Map seasonal feed scarcity by comparing monthly feed availability against livestock requirements
Map land availability using cropland area per person
Map water availability using precipitation and flow accumulation data
Overlay the four maps to create 16 domains using random forest clustering
Select contrasting domains for analysis
Apply FEAST/Techfit logic to predict suitable feed interventions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Analysis used 10 years of data (2008-2017)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Net Primary Productivity data, land cover data, cattle density, population density, crop area data, rainfall distribution maps, livestock production systems data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: ESRI ArcGIS 10.4, R software, GIS expertise
what_youll_need.human_resources: GIS analysts, livestock systems researchers, data analysts
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Access to spatial datasets from multiple sources (Copernicus, FAO, ILRI, WorldPop, etc.)
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Spatial maps showing 16 domains of feed constraints and system characteristics, recommendations for suitable feed interventions per domain
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved targeting of climate-resilient feed interventions to enhance livestock productivity in mixed farming systems
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Study identified four contrasting domains with specific feed intervention recommendations, but implementation and effectiveness testing not conducted

practical_examples.location: Domain 2 (Kenya: Kitui, Mkueni, Embu counties; Tanzania: Morogoro region)
practical_examples.description: Areas with very high feed quantity but 3 months seasonal scarcity, low land availability, high water availability
practical_examples.key_result: Recommended irrigated fodder production combined with energy-rich supplements like molasses

practical_examples.location: Domain 16 (Uganda: multiple districts including Kampala, Wakiso)
practical_examples.description: Areas with very low feed quantity and 12 months scarcity, very low land availability
practical_examples.key_result: Recommended rehabilitation of degraded grazing land and purchased crop residues or hay

10568-100247.limitations_and_considerations: Study focused on only two feed constraints and two system characteristics from FEAST while the full approach includes three constraints and six characteristics. Water availability was defined as precipitation rather than access to standing water due to data limitations. Only cattle density was considered, not other livestock types.
key_takeaways: Feed scarcity is a major constraint to livestock productivity across East African mixed farming systems, with Uganda showing the most severe constraints
Land availability for forage production is significantly limited across all domains, likely due to land degradation and competing uses
Different domains require different feed intervention strategies based on their specific constraints and system characteristics
Integration of multiple feed interventions is necessary to address all constraints within a domain
Policy interventions are needed to support land rehabilitation, rainwater harvesting, and farmer access to feed technologies

source_and_citation.full_citation: Fakude, M. (2018). Targeting new climate-resilient feeds and forages in the smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa. Master's thesis, NUI Galway.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: NUI Galway / International Livestock Research Institute (ILRI)

10568-100247._title_meta: Targeting new climate-resilient feeds and forages in the smallholder mixed farming systems of East Africa

10568-100257.overview: This is an academic research paper that investigates how temperature affects the development, survival, and reproduction of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) larvae when reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya - cow dung and brewers' spent grain. The study aims to optimize black soldier fly production systems for sustainable livestock feed production in sub-Saharan Africa.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a peer-reviewed research article published in Animals journal, despite being labeled as 'Tool' in metadata

classification.development_stage_evidence: The study was conducted in laboratories with controlled experiments using different temperature regimes and substrates, with the stock colony maintained for over 8 generations before use

who_its_for.primary_audience: Researchers, entomologists, and agricultural scientists studying insect-based feed production
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, universities, agricultural development organizations, and insect farming enterprises

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Urban environments with available organic waste streams

how_it_works.approach_summary: The study used controlled laboratory experiments to test black soldier fly larvae development on two substrates (cow dung and brewers' spent grain) at five different temperatures (15°C, 20°C, 25°C, 30°C, and 35°C), measuring development time, weight, survival, and reproductive success.
key_steps: Collect eggs from stock colony and hatch larvae
Place 100 3-5 day old larvae in containers with test substrates at 70% moisture
Subject containers to different temperature treatments in incubators
Monitor daily and record larval development duration
Remove prepupae, weigh, and place in sawdust for pupation
Monitor adult emergence and record pupal development time
Allow adults to mate and record longevity and fecundity
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Development time varied from 24-239 days depending on temperature and substrate
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Fresh cow dung and brewers' spent grain, controlled temperature facilities, black soldier fly stock colony

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Controlled temperature incubators, laboratory facilities, microscopes for egg counting
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained researchers with entomology expertise
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Research laboratory with insectary facilities
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Data on optimal temperature ranges and substrate types for black soldier fly production
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved black soldier fly rearing methods for sustainable livestock feed production in sub-Saharan Africa
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: BSF larvae reared on spent grain developed faster and had higher survival rates, with optimal temperatures of 25-30°C for development

practical_examples.description: Laboratory experiments conducted at the International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology using locally sourced cow dung and brewers' spent grain
practical_examples.key_result: Spent grain-fed larvae developed 53-88% faster than cow dung-fed larvae at temperatures between 20-35°C

10568-100257.limitations_and_considerations: The study was conducted under controlled laboratory conditions and may not reflect field conditions. The research focused on only two substrate types and did not test substrate hygiene, pathogen contamination, or heavy metal bioaccumulation. Results may vary with different black soldier fly strains or environmental conditions.
key_takeaways: Black soldier fly larvae reared on brewers' spent grain outperformed those on cow dung in terms of development speed and weight gain
Optimal temperature range for BSF development is 25-30°C regardless of substrate type
Higher temperatures reduce adult longevity but increase fecundity
Substrate nutritional quality significantly affects all life-history traits of black soldier flies
Urban organic waste streams like spent grain can be effectively used for sustainable insect protein production

source_and_citation.full_citation: Shumo, M.; Khamis, F.M.; Tanga, C.M.; Fiaboe, K.K.M.; Subramanian, S.; Ekesi, S.; Huis, A. van; Borgemeister, C. (2019). Influence of temperature on selected life-history traits of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya. Animals, 9(3), 79.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: International Centre of Insect Physiology and Ecology (icipe)

10568-100257._title_meta: Influence of temperature on selected life-history traits of black soldier fly (Hermetia illucens) reared on two common urban organic waste streams in Kenya

10568-100263.overview: The Climate-Smart Village (CSV) AR4D Approach is a comprehensive framework developed by CCAFS for testing, evaluating, and scaling climate-smart agricultural practices through participatory action research. This synthesis document captures four years of lessons learned from implementing CSVs across 35 sites in 20 countries, providing evidence-based guidance for scaling climate-smart agriculture interventions.

resource_type.type_notes: Document presents a structured approach with principles, implementation guidelines, and lessons learned from a multi-site research framework

classification.development_stage_evidence: As of end of 2017, a total of 35 AR4D CSVs have been established and are being actively managed by CCAFS and partners, covering 20 countries across all 5 CCAFS priority regions

who_its_for.primary_audience: Agricultural researchers, development practitioners, policymakers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on climate-smart agriculture
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, development organizations, government agencies, and international agricultural programs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Multiple agro-ecosystems including rice-wheat systems, dairy systems, groundnut basin, and various smallholder farming contexts

how_it_works.approach_summary: CSVs combine bottom-up participatory action research with top-down science-led approaches to test portfolios of climate-smart agricultural interventions. The approach focuses on generating evidence of CSA effectiveness in real-life settings and co-developing scaling mechanisms towards landscape, subnational and national levels.
key_steps: Establish benchmark sites based on biophysical and socio-economic context
Engage multi-stakeholder partnerships for participatory research
Test portfolios of tailored technical and institutional CSA interventions
Generate science-based evidence on CSA effectiveness across productivity, adaptation, and mitigation outcomes
Develop adaptation domains to facilitate transferability
Identify business models and incentive mechanisms for scaling
Scale out successful interventions through policy processes and development programs
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-year implementation with Phase I covering 2011-2017
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Quantitative data on costs/benefits, climate data, socio-economic baseline data, and monitoring data across three CSA pillars

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Capacity for participatory action research, climate data collection and analysis, economic valuation methods, and monitoring systems
what_youll_need.human_resources: Multi-disciplinary teams including agricultural researchers, social scientists, extension agents, and local implementing partners
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Multi-stakeholder partnerships involving research institutions, government agencies, NGOs, and private sector actors
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Significant multi-year funding required, with examples showing government investments of millions of dollars for scaling

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Science-based evidence on CSA effectiveness, adaptation domains for transferability, business models for scaling, and documented best practices
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Wide adoption of climate-smart agricultural practices leading to enhanced productivity, increased incomes, climate resilience, and mitigation benefits
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Documented outcomes include thousands of villages adopting CSV approaches, millions of farmers reached through scaling initiatives, and significant government investments in CSA programs

practical_examples.description: Private sector (ITC Limited) developed about 2000 climate smart villages in 6 states, and the Government announced a multi-billion-dollar scheme for solar irrigation based on the SPICE model tested in CSVs
practical_examples.key_result: National-scale policy adoption and private sector engagement

practical_examples.description: National government began rolling out the CSV approach as part of key policies for 2016-17 to create linkages between farmers, government and private sector
practical_examples.key_result: Government mainstreaming of CSV approach in national policy

practical_examples.description: State Government of Cauca approved USD$66 million from the Royalties National Fund to scale CSA in at least 8 municipalities based on CSV evidence
practical_examples.key_result: Major government investment in scaling CSA practices

practical_examples.description: Climate-smart dairy practices disseminated among 600,000 farmers through 6 producers' organizations, with 25% women participation
practical_examples.key_result: Large-scale adoption of improved livestock practices

10568-100263.limitations_and_considerations: Key challenges include avoiding technological bias towards 'rich' farmers, ensuring sustainability of resource-intensive options beyond project cycles, need for sound quantitative evidence collection, addressing market linkages and adoption incentives, and concentrating resources on sites with multi-level institutional buy-in for expected outcomes.
key_takeaways: CSVs are testing grounds for building science-based evidence around Climate-Smart Agricultural portfolios to support integration into government policies and scaling mechanisms
One size does not fit all - context-specific theories of change and implementation mechanisms are needed for success
Balance between technology and socio-economic aspects is crucial, with attention to gender, youth, and indigenous knowledge systems
Strong quantitative data collection is essential to support scaling strategies that require cost/benefit evidence
Success requires multi-level institutional buy-in and concentration of resources on sites likely to deliver high-level outcomes through scale out

source_and_citation.full_citation: CCAFS (2018). Climate-Smart Village AR4D Approach: Synthesis of lessons learned. Wageningen, Netherlands: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

10568-100263._title_meta: Climate-Smart Village AR4D Approach: Synthesis of lessons learned

10568-100264.overview: This resource documents lessons learned from a partnership between NGOs, government, and research organizations in Zambia to develop mobile device-based solutions for collecting food and nutrition security data. It presents findings from pilot tests of voice call surveys and rapid mobile surveys to address the need for more comprehensive, faster, and cheaper monitoring and evaluation approaches.

resource_type.type_notes: While metadata labels it as 'Tool', the content is clearly a case study documenting what worked and didn't work in pilot implementations

classification.development_stage_evidence: Results from pilots show both promising results and challenges during the transition to rapid and mobile-based surveys

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, government officials, and researchers working in food and nutrition security
who_its_for.organizational_context: NGOs, government agencies, research institutions, and development programs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Rural areas with mobile phone penetration

how_it_works.approach_summary: The approach involves using mobile technology for data collection through voice calls and rapid surveys to monitor food and nutrition security indicators more frequently and cost-effectively than traditional face-to-face methods.
key_steps: Conduct initial face-to-face interviews with program participants
Collect phone numbers for follow-up callbacks
Train local enumerators to conduct voice call surveys using smartphones and ODK Collect
Implement callback surveys approximately two weeks after initial interviews
Use rapid Rural Household Multi-Indicator Survey (RHoMIS) for impact evaluation
Analyze data to identify program effectiveness and areas for improvement
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Callbacks conducted approximately two weeks after initial interviews, with follow-up calls completed within a one-week period
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Phone numbers of participants, trained enumerators, smartphones with ODK Collect, network connectivity

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Smartphones, ODK Collect software, reliable network connectivity
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained local enumerators, oversight staff for call centers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership between research, development, and government organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Lower cost than traditional face-to-face surveys, though specific costs not provided

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: High-frequency monitoring data on dietary diversity, knowledge attitudes and practices (KAP), and WASH practices
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: More comprehensive, faster, and cheaper monitoring and evaluation for food and nutrition security programs
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: 89% callback success rate among those providing phone numbers; statistically significant differences detected in WASH practices between beneficiaries and non-beneficiaries

practical_examples.description: Voice call surveys for KAP monitoring achieved 89% callback success rate among participants who provided phone numbers
practical_examples.key_result: Of 189 face-to-face participants, 107 provided phone numbers and 95 were successfully reached via callbacks

practical_examples.description: FANSER program used rapid surveys to evaluate WASH practice improvements after 1.5 years of implementation
practical_examples.key_result: Statistically significant differences found in sanitation practices between beneficiaries and non-beneficiaries

practical_examples.description: SUN program used rapid KAP surveys to identify most effective information channels
practical_examples.key_result: 80% of respondents received information from Community Health Workers; radio and women's empowerment groups were most effective within SUN programs

10568-100264.limitations_and_considerations: Only 56% of participants were willing and able to provide phone numbers for follow-up; challenges included lack of phone ownership, shared phones, unwillingness to participate, wrong numbers, and network connectivity issues. Technical, financial, and programmatic barriers were identified that preclude achieving the goal of collecting more data for less money.
key_takeaways: Mobile-based surveys offer opportunities to track key indicators over time and reduce monitoring costs
Voice calls outperform other mobile survey options when clarification is needed and questionnaires are longer than a few questions
Success rates for callbacks are high (89%) among those willing to provide phone numbers, but only 56% of participants were willing to do so
Rapid surveys can effectively detect program impacts and identify areas for programming focus
Further innovation and addressing technical, financial, and programmatic barriers are needed for wider implementation

source_and_citation.full_citation: Chesterman, S., Lamanna, C., Hammond, J., Namoi, N., Wakelin, O., Ng'endo, M., van Wijk, M.T., & Rosenstock, T.S. (2017). Co-designed solutions for collecting food & nutrition security data with mobile devices: What worked (and did not) from a NGO, government and research partnership. CCAFS Info Note.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

10568-100264._title_meta: Co-designed solutions for collecting food & nutrition security data with mobile devices: What worked (and did not) from a NGO, government and research partnership

10568-100289.overview: Spore 192 is a quarterly magazine issue focused on digitalizing agriculture to bridge the gender gap. It explores how digital technologies are providing women farmers with better access to finance, information, and markets, while opening opportunities for young entrepreneurs in agribusiness across Africa, Caribbean, and Pacific regions.

resource_type.type_notes: Magazine format but functions as a comprehensive report with analysis, case studies, and field reports on agricultural digitalization

classification.development_stage_evidence: Multiple examples show technologies already in use: 'Since its launch in December 2018, more than 100 farmers have signed up to Binkabi' and 'SmartFarm technology has since been scaled out to 500,000 farming entities'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Development organizations, agricultural research institutions, government agencies, and private sector companies working in agricultural innovation

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Diverse contexts from arid regions to tropical zones, focusing on smallholder farming systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: The magazine presents a comprehensive analysis of how digital technologies can bridge gender gaps in agriculture through improved access to services, markets, and information. It combines policy analysis with practical case studies and field reports.
key_steps: Identify gender-specific barriers in agricultural value chains
Implement digital solutions targeting women's needs
Provide training and capacity building for technology adoption
Create supportive policy frameworks
Build networks and platforms for women agripreneurs
Monitor and evaluate impact on gender equality
Scale successful interventions across regions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Ongoing initiatives with various implementation timelines
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Gender-disaggregated data, technology access information, market data, policy analysis

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Mobile technology infrastructure, internet connectivity, digital platforms and applications
what_youll_need.human_resources: Technical specialists, gender experts, field coordinators, trainers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships between government, private sector, and development organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Varies by intervention, from €175-525 for soil sensors to millions for comprehensive programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Increased women's access to digital agricultural services, improved market linkages, enhanced financial inclusion
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Reduced gender gap in agriculture, increased productivity and incomes for women farmers, strengthened food security
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Women farmers increased annual incomes by €223 through ReelFruit program; women's profits increased by 82% in Ghana shea nut value chain through digital technology

practical_examples.description: Shea nut farmers using digital technology to connect directly with international markets through Shea Network Ghana
practical_examples.key_result: Women's profits increased by 82% due to access to new international buyers' market

practical_examples.description: Zenvus precision farming sensors providing soil data to 500,000 farming entities
practical_examples.key_result: Farmers able to apply correct fertilizer and optimize irrigation, leading to enhanced productivity

practical_examples.description: Lawyers 4 Farmers SMS service providing legal advice to 3,000 farmers, 70% of whom are women
practical_examples.key_result: Successfully resolved 264 legal cases in 2018, helping women secure land rights

10568-100289.limitations_and_considerations: Infrastructure and accessibility of technology remain biggest challenges; digital literacy barriers; need for supportive policy frameworks; risk of further marginalizing women without proper implementation; cost barriers for smallholder farmers; cultural barriers to technology adoption
key_takeaways: Digital technologies can significantly reduce gender gaps in agriculture when properly implemented with supportive policies
Women farmers show strong adoption rates when digital solutions address their specific needs and constraints
Success requires holistic approaches combining technology, training, policy support, and institutional partnerships
Mobile-based solutions are particularly effective in reaching rural women farmers across Africa
Evidence shows substantial income increases and improved market access when women gain digital access to agricultural services

source_and_citation.full_citation: Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation (2019). Spore 192: Digitalising agriculture - Bridging the gender gap. CTA.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation (CTA)

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Very comprehensive document with multiple articles and case studies providing extensive evidence and examples

10568-100302.overview: This is an academic review paper that explores the application of genetic engineering approaches to control bacterial wilt disease in enset (Ensete ventricosum), Ethiopia's indigenous sustainability crop that supports 20 million people. The paper discusses the devastating impact of Xanthomonas campestris pv. musacearum on enset production and reviews genetic engineering solutions successfully applied to banana that could be adapted for enset.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a comprehensive review article published in Frontiers in Plant Science examining genetic engineering approaches for disease control

classification.development_stage_evidence: There is no report on genetic improvement of enset via neither conventional breeding nor biotechnology applications for any trait including bacterial wilt resistance... Therefore, it is a prerequisite to develop a transformation system for cultivated enset cultivars

who_its_for.primary_audience: Plant biotechnologists, agricultural researchers, and scientists working on crop improvement
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural research centers, biotechnology companies, and academic institutions

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Enset-growing regions in Southern and Southwestern Ethiopia, densely populated areas with small landholdings

how_it_works.approach_summary: The paper proposes developing genetic transformation systems for enset to introduce bacterial wilt resistance genes (such as Hrap and Pflp) that have been successfully used in banana. This would involve establishing tissue culture protocols, transformation methods, and regeneration systems for enset cultivars.
key_steps: Develop transformation and regeneration protocols for enset cultivars
Introduce resistance genes (Hrap, Pflp, or Xa21) via genetic transformation
Screen transgenic lines for bacterial wilt resistance
Evaluate agronomic performance of resistant lines
Conduct field trials under various agro-ecological conditions
Scale up production of disease-free planting material
Deploy resistant varieties to farmers
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Several years for development and testing, considering enset's long generation time of 9-14 years
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Enset tissue culture protocols, transformation vectors with resistance genes, bacterial strains for testing, greenhouse and field trial facilities

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Tissue culture facilities, genetic transformation equipment, molecular biology tools, greenhouse and field trial infrastructure
what_youll_need.human_resources: Plant biotechnologists, molecular biologists, plant pathologists, tissue culture specialists
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Research institutions with biotechnology capabilities, regulatory approval for transgenic crops, collaboration between international and national research centers
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but requires significant investment in biotechnology infrastructure and multi-year research programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Bacterial wilt resistant enset varieties, improved tissue culture and transformation protocols for enset, disease-free planting material
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Reduced crop losses from bacterial wilt disease, improved food security for 20 million people dependent on enset, preservation of enset genetic diversity
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Success demonstrated in banana with transgenic lines showing complete resistance to X. campestris pv. musacearum in greenhouse and field trials

practical_examples.description: Transgenic banana lines expressing Hrap or Pflp genes showed complete resistance against X. campestris pv. musacearum in greenhouse tests and enhanced resistance in confined field trials for two successive cropping cycles.
practical_examples.key_result: Normal agronomic performance and yield comparable to control nontransgenic plants while maintaining disease resistance

10568-100302.limitations_and_considerations: Long generation time of enset (9-14 years) makes traditional breeding unattractive. No transformation and regeneration system currently available for enset. Regulatory approval needed for transgenic crops. Potential for pathogen evolution to break single-gene resistance, requiring gene stacking approaches. Need for extensive screening under various agro-ecological conditions due to pathogen strain variability.
key_takeaways: Bacterial wilt disease caused by Xanthomonas campestris pv. musacearum is the most devastating constraint to enset production, with no known resistant varieties available
Genetic engineering approaches successfully used in banana for bacterial wilt resistance could be adapted for enset, as both crops are affected by the same pathogen
Development of transformation and regeneration protocols for enset cultivars is a prerequisite for successful genetic engineering applications
Gene stacking approaches using multiple resistance genes (Hrap, Pflp, Xa21) may provide more durable resistance than single gene strategies
Integration of biotechnology approaches with improved phytosanitary practices and clean planting material systems is essential for comprehensive disease management

source_and_citation.full_citation: Merga, I.F., Tripathi, L., Hvoslef-Eide, A.K., & Gebre, E. (2019). Application of Genetic Engineering for Control of Bacterial Wilt Disease of Enset, Ethiopia's Sustainability Crop. Frontiers in Plant Science, 10, 133.

10568-100302._title_meta: Application of genetic engineering for control of bacterial wilt disease of enset, Ethiopia's sustainability crop

10568-100304.overview: This report documents lessons learned from a South-South exchange workshop between three Latin American cities (Cali, Quito, and Medellín) on building sustainable urban food systems. It analyzes different approaches these cities have taken to address food security challenges and provides recommendations for developing comprehensive food policies within a city-region framework.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a comprehensive report documenting findings from a workshop and analyzing case studies, not a framework or guidelines as suggested in metadata

classification.development_stage_evidence: Quito has successfully spent more than 16 years consolidating family- and community-based urban farming practices

who_its_for.primary_audience: Urban planners, policymakers, and development practitioners working on sustainable food systems
who_its_for.organizational_context: Municipal governments, development organizations, research institutions

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Urban and peri-urban environments in intermediate cities

how_it_works.approach_summary: The approach involves analyzing different city experiences with sustainable food systems, identifying common challenges, and facilitating knowledge exchange through South-South cooperation to develop comprehensive food policies.
key_steps: Assess local food system challenges and starting points
Develop city-specific interventions (urban agriculture, nutrition programs, supply chain improvements)
Engage multiple stakeholders across sectors
Formulate comprehensive food policies
Establish monitoring indicators
Align with international frameworks like Milan Urban Food Policy Pact
Create mechanisms for ongoing South-South exchange
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Long-term process spanning multiple years (Quito's experience spans 16+ years)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Food system mapping, nutritional status data, supply chain analysis, stakeholder mapping

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Food system monitoring indicators, data collection systems
what_youll_need.human_resources: Multi-sectoral teams including health, agriculture, urban planning, and community development professionals
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Municipal government commitment, inter-sectoral coordination mechanisms, stakeholder engagement platforms
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive food policies, monitoring systems, stakeholder engagement platforms, short supply chains
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved food security, enhanced nutrition, sustainable urban development, strengthened urban-rural linkages
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Quito's urban agriculture program became a sustainability indicator for the city and integrated into climate action and resilience plans

practical_examples.description: 16-year urban agriculture program focusing on social inclusion that evolved into comprehensive food system planning
practical_examples.key_result: Program became city sustainability indicator and led to adherence to Milan Urban Food Policy Pact

practical_examples.description: Health-focused approach addressing double nutritional burden through various interventions including healthy school kiosks
practical_examples.key_result: Development of comprehensive Food and Nutritional Security and Sovereignty Policy proposal

practical_examples.description: Alliance for Good Living program addressing urban-rural food security gap through supply system improvements
practical_examples.key_result: Reduced intermediation channels between producers and consumers while promoting local food consumption

10568-100304.limitations_and_considerations: Common challenges include understanding food systems complexity, integrating food security into urban planning, mobilizing diverse actors, institutionalizing policies, implementing monitoring systems, aligning with international frameworks, overcoming information gaps, and accounting for long development timelines.
key_takeaways: Different cities can start from various entry points (social inclusion, health, supply chains) but converge on comprehensive food system approaches
South-South exchanges generate cooperation bonds and inspire innovation among similar cities
Sustainable food systems create ideal spaces for strengthening collaborative work between actors and sectors
Urban-rural alliances are key tools for integral development promoting short supply chains
Public policies on food sovereignty and security are fundamental commitments for social investment and development

source_and_citation.full_citation: Rankin, Sara; Bonilla Findji, Osana (2019). What can the Latin American cities of Cali, Quito and Medellín learn from each other's efforts to build sustainable food systems? CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Water, Land and Ecosystems
