10568-100309.overview: This presentation describes the ILRI Genebank's forage collection and its role in developing improved forage varieties to address feed constraints in smallholder livestock systems. It showcases how genetic resources, combined with modern breeding techniques and nutritional analysis, can help develop drought-resistant and high-yielding forage varieties for tropical and subtropical regions.

resource_type.type_notes: Clear presentation format with slides covering ILRI's forage improvement work

classification.development_stage_evidence: Smut and stunt disease resistant lines identified from the in trust collection at ILRI and being adopted by farmers

who_its_for.primary_audience: Plant breeders, livestock researchers, and agricultural development practitioners
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural development organizations, and breeding programs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical and subtropical regions with varying altitudes (1650-2400 m.a.s.l.) and rainfall patterns (500-1200mm)

how_it_works.approach_summary: The ILRI Genebank maintains diverse forage collections across four field sites with different agro-ecological conditions, uses modern breeding techniques including Marker Assisted Selection, and provides nutritional analysis through NIRS technology to develop improved forage varieties.
key_steps: Germplasm characterization and evaluation at field genebanks
Identification of promising genotypes using breeding techniques
Nutritional analysis using Near Infrared Spectroscopy (NIRS)
Variety registration and basic seed production
Delivery to producers through public and private sector partnerships
On-farm trials and farmer field testing
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-year breeding and evaluation process
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Genetic material from genebank collections, nutritional analysis data, agronomic performance data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: NIRS equipment for nutritional analysis (~$60,000), field genebank facilities, molecular breeding capabilities
what_youll_need.human_resources: Plant breeders, nutritionists, field technicians, extension specialists
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Research facilities, field sites across different agro-ecological zones, partnerships with national programs
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Significant investment in equipment, facilities, and long-term breeding programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Improved forage varieties with enhanced drought tolerance, disease resistance, and nutritional quality
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased livestock productivity, improved soil fertility through nitrogen fixation, reduced erosion, and enhanced system resilience
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: High yielding Napier grass lines produce 5 times more biomass than natural pastures in Tanzania; milk yield increases of 57% for local cows and 78% for crossbreds with oat-vetch supplementation

practical_examples.description: High yielding Napier grass lines from ILRI genebank collection
practical_examples.key_result: Produced 5 times more biomass than natural pastures

practical_examples.description: Oat-vetch supplementation trials in highland areas
practical_examples.key_result: Increased milk yields by 57% for local cows and 78% for crossbreds

practical_examples.description: Desho grass adoption in highlands with natural resource management practices
practical_examples.key_result: Widely used for forage and cash income through trading of roots and biomass

10568-100309.limitations_and_considerations: Forage adoption has been slow and is improved when linked to market opportunities, matched to production system niches, easy to manage, supported by strong partnerships and enabling policies.
key_takeaways: The ILRI Genebank maintains 18,635 forage accessions representing 1,400 species across four field sites with different agro-ecological conditions
Modern breeding techniques including Marker Assisted Selection and NIRS nutritional analysis can accelerate development of improved forage varieties
Successful forage adoption requires integration with market opportunities, natural resource management, and strong institutional partnerships
Improved forages can significantly increase livestock productivity while providing environmental benefits like soil fertility improvement and erosion control
Strong public and private investment in the feed sector and enabling policies are needed to meet growing feed demands

source_and_citation.full_citation: Jones, Christopher S.; Sartie, Alieu (2018). Opportunities for forage improvement through the ILRI Genebank. ILRI.

10568-100309._title_meta: Opportunities for forage improvement through the ILRI Genebank

10568-100320.overview: This is a comprehensive academic chapter that provides strategic recommendations for enhancing the conservation and utilization of North America's native plant genetic resources, including crop wild relatives and underutilized species. It addresses the challenge of realizing the agricultural and nutritional potential of thousands of North American plant species through improved conservation strategies, research investment, and market development.

resource_type.type_notes: This is Chapter 20 from a Springer academic book, providing research-based analysis and recommendations

classification.development_stage_evidence: The chapter outlines 'some of the key steps needed to boost the conservation and use of our regional cornucopia' and discusses what 'could be taken' and 'should be prioritized'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Researchers, policymakers, conservation practitioners, and plant breeders working on genetic resources
who_its_for.organizational_context: Academic institutions, government agencies, genebanks, botanic gardens, conservation organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Temperate and subtropical regions of North America with diverse native plant species

how_it_works.approach_summary: The framework proposes a comprehensive approach combining in situ and ex situ conservation with enhanced research, pre-breeding, and marketing efforts to realize the potential of North American plant genetic resources.
key_steps: Improve documentation and monitoring of wild plant populations in protected areas
Enhance ex situ conservation through coordinated information sharing and gap analysis
Strengthen characterization and evaluation of conserved genetic resources
Invest in pre-breeding and breeding programs for underutilized species
Develop marketing strategies for new crops and varieties
Foster cross-institutional and international collaboration
Build public awareness and support for plant genetic resource conservation
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Taxonomic and floristic information, occurrence data, ecogeographic data, characterization and evaluation data from genebanks

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Information management systems, molecular tools for characterization, ecogeographic modeling capabilities
what_youll_need.human_resources: Field botanists, conservation practitioners, plant breeders, data managers, marketing specialists
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Coordination between genebanks, botanic gardens, protected area managers, research institutions, and government agencies
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Significant investment needed for staffing, information technologies, and research programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Enhanced conservation of North American plant genetic resources, improved documentation and accessibility of collections, new crop varieties and products
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: More diverse, nutritious, and sustainable food and agricultural systems utilizing North America's native plant diversity
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Examples cited include successful use of wild sunflower relatives for pest resistance, wild grape contributions to phylloxera resistance, and market expansion of quinoa and chia

practical_examples.description: Wild sunflower relatives have been extensively used in crop breeding for pest and disease resistance traits
practical_examples.key_result: Significant contributions to commercial sunflower improvement

practical_examples.description: North American wild grapes provided resistance to phylloxera in European rootstocks
practical_examples.key_result: Saved European wine industry from devastating pest

practical_examples.description: Recent expansion of quinoa and chia demonstrates potential for crop diversification
practical_examples.key_result: Evidence that new crops can gain market acceptance with proper development

10568-100320.limitations_and_considerations: Major challenges include habitat destruction threatening wild populations, insufficient funding for conservation and research programs, complex international policies on genetic resource sharing, technical difficulties in conserving wild species ex situ, and the lengthy process of developing new crops from wild species.
key_takeaways: North America possesses thousands of potentially valuable plant species that are currently underutilized
Coordinated conservation efforts combining in situ and ex situ approaches are essential for preserving genetic diversity
Investment in pre-breeding and breeding programs is needed to develop underutilized species into productive crops
Cross-institutional collaboration and information sharing are critical for effective conservation and utilization
Market development and public awareness are necessary to create demand for new crops and products from native species

source_and_citation.full_citation: Khoury, C.K., Greene, S.L., Williams, K.A., Kantar, M.B., and Marek, L.F. (2018). Conservation and Use of the North American Plant Cornucopia: The Way Forward. In: Greene SL, Williams KA, Khoury CK, Kantar MB, and Marek LF, eds., North American Crop Wild Relatives, Volume 2: Important Species. Springer.

10568-100320._title_meta: Conservation and Use of the North American Plant Cornucopia: The Way Forward

10568-100324.overview: This working paper presents a gender assessment of three agricultural innovations developed by ResilientAfrica Network (RAN) to evaluate their gender-responsiveness for climate-smart agriculture. The study examines ownership patterns, usage, decision-making processes, and benefits of innovations including a low-cost solar irrigation pump, mushroom cultivation using crop residues, and M-Omulimisa mobile extension platform among farmers in Northern Uganda.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a research report presenting findings from a gender assessment study, though metadata labels it as Framework/Guidelines

classification.development_stage_evidence: The innovations 'had been tested in several communities in Uganda' and farmers in Central Uganda 'have already adopted this approach enabling them to grow mushrooms regularly'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and innovation managers working on agricultural technology adoption
who_its_for.organizational_context: CGIAR centers, agricultural research institutions, NGOs, and innovation networks

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Rural agricultural communities in Northern Uganda with challenges of prolonged dry spells and limited extension services

how_it_works.approach_summary: The assessment used qualitative research methods including focus group discussions, in-depth interviews, and key informant interviews to evaluate gender-responsiveness of three agricultural innovations using Climate Smart Agriculture framework.
key_steps: Selection of three agricultural innovations based on CSA focus and affordability criteria
Training of research assistants and pre-testing of assessment tools
Demonstration of innovations to farmers before data collection
Conduct sex-disaggregated focus group discussions, in-depth interviews, and key informant interviews
Thematic analysis of transcripts using study objectives framework
Validation of findings through co-creation workshops and community dissemination meetings
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Field assessment conducted over several days with 2-day training for research assistants
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Access to innovation teams for demonstrations, trained research assistants, consent forms, audio recording equipment

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Audio recorders, flip charts, colored markers, demonstration materials for innovations
what_youll_need.human_resources: Ten trained research assistants, innovation teams, moderators familiar with gender issues and participatory methods
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with local organizations having established networks with farmers, ethical approval for research
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but includes costs for research assistants, materials, venue hire, and travel for innovation teams

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Gender assessment report with findings on ownership, usage, decision-making patterns, and recommendations for improving gender-responsiveness of agricultural innovations
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced gender-responsive design and implementation of climate-smart agricultural innovations leading to more equitable access and benefits for male and female farmers
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Study provided practical recommendations including need for gender sensitization, group ownership models, grants for women farmers, and community demonstration sites

practical_examples.location: Northern Uganda (Apac, Kole, Lira, Oyam districts)
practical_examples.description: Assessment of three RAN innovations with 128 respondents (74 females, 54 males) using participatory methods
practical_examples.key_result: Found that men would own technology-based innovations while women would own food production innovations, highlighting need for gender-sensitive design

10568-100324.limitations_and_considerations: Cultural norms strongly influenced ownership and usage patterns regardless of innovation benefits. Some respondents uncomfortable discussing gender issues publicly. Age-related concerns about young interviewers. Literacy challenges required pairing of respondents. Limited access to demonstration inputs in rural settings.
key_takeaways: Socio-cultural norms and gender roles significantly influence adoption of agricultural innovations regardless of their technical benefits
Men typically control ownership of technology-based innovations while women are expected to use them more, creating potential for increased work burden
Decision-making remains male-dominated even when women report joint decisions, affecting women's access to innovation benefits
Gender sensitization and community demonstration sites are essential for promoting equitable adoption of climate-smart agricultural innovations
Financial access mechanisms like Village Saving Loan Associations and grants are needed to enable women farmers to afford innovations

source_and_citation.full_citation: Okello, D., Mayega, R.W., Muhumuza, C., Amuge, P.O., Kakamagi, E., Amollo, M., Amuka, I., Kayiwa, R., & Bazeyo, W. (2018). Gender and innovation for climate-smart agriculture: Assessment of gender-responsiveness of RAN's agricultural-focused innovations. CCAFS Working Paper no. 260. CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Some formatting artifacts and repeated text sections, but content is comprehensive and well-structured

10568-100324._title_meta: Gender and innovation for climate-smart agriculture. Assessment of gender-responsiveness of RAN's agricultural-focused innovations

10568-100325.overview: This is a comprehensive country profile that provides a baseline assessment of Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) in Malawi, analyzing the current state of agricultural systems, climate vulnerabilities, CSA practices, institutional frameworks, and financing mechanisms. It serves as a foundational resource for identifying entry points and investment opportunities for scaling CSA interventions in Malawi's agriculture sector.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive country assessment document providing detailed analysis and baseline data

classification.development_stage_evidence: There are about 308 CSA-related projects currently being implemented nationally; only circa 14% are exclusively for CSA

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural development and climate change in Malawi
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, international development organizations, research institutions, NGOs, and donor agencies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Three agro-ecological zones: Lower Shire valley, lakeshore plains and Upper Shire valley, and mid-altitude plateau with highlands

how_it_works.approach_summary: The profile uses a comprehensive assessment methodology examining agricultural production systems, climate vulnerabilities, CSA practices, institutional frameworks, and financing mechanisms to create a baseline for CSA investment planning.
key_steps: Analyze agricultural production systems key for food security
Assess climate change impacts and vulnerabilities
Evaluate existing CSA practices and their climate-smartness
Map institutional landscape and policy frameworks
Assess financing mechanisms and opportunities
Identify entry points for CSA scaling
Provide recommendations for investment priorities
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Baseline assessment conducted in 2018
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Agricultural statistics, climate data, policy documents, institutional mapping, project inventories, expert consultations

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Climate data analysis capabilities, agricultural systems knowledge, policy analysis expertise
what_youll_need.human_resources: Multi-disciplinary team including agricultural economists, climate scientists, policy analysts, and local experts
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Collaboration with government agencies, research institutions, and development partners
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive baseline assessment, identification of CSA investment priorities, mapping of institutional landscape, analysis of financing gaps and opportunities
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved coordination and targeting of CSA investments, enhanced policy coherence, increased adoption of climate-smart practices
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Document provides baseline data showing low CSA adoption rates (1-2% for Conservation Agriculture, 4% irrigated land) indicating need for targeted interventions

practical_examples.description: The Enhancing Community Resilience to Climate Change and Variability Project (ECRP) implemented comprehensive CSA interventions over 5 years
practical_examples.key_result: Food security more than doubled (from 15% to 35% of families with 9+ months food supply), average household incomes doubled, and 166,522 individuals adopted multiple CSA techniques

10568-100325.limitations_and_considerations: Major challenges include weak coordination among stakeholders resulting in duplication of efforts, low adoption rates of CSA practices due to knowledge gaps and resource constraints, gender disparities in access to resources, land tenure insecurity, and insufficient climate financing awareness and access.
key_takeaways: Agriculture accounts for 30% of Malawi's GDP and supports 90% of the population, making climate resilience critical for national development
CSA adoption remains extremely low (1-2% for Conservation Agriculture) despite 308 ongoing projects, indicating coordination and implementation challenges
Climate change will significantly impact agricultural productivity, with maize and potato yields projected to decline by 10.61% and 12.78% respectively by 2050
Major institutional and financing gaps exist, with over 60% of available climate funds unknown to stakeholders and weak capacity for proposal development
Successful CSA scaling requires addressing gender disparities, land tenure issues, and improving coordination among the 70+ institutions involved in CSA activities

source_and_citation.full_citation: International Center for Tropical Agriculture; World Bank (2018). Climate-Smart Agriculture in Malawi. CSA Country Profiles for Africa Series.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Bank

10568-100326.overview: This is a comprehensive country profile that analyzes climate-smart agriculture (CSA) opportunities in Belize, identifying promising practices, institutional frameworks, and financing strategies to help the agricultural sector adapt to climate change while increasing productivity and reducing greenhouse gas emissions.

resource_type.type_notes: While metadata lists both Training Manual and Framework, the content is clearly a comprehensive analytical framework for assessing and implementing CSA in Belize

classification.development_stage_evidence: Many farmers in Belize are already using CSA measures to some degree... promising CSA practices that are being promoted include construction of drainage and irrigation infrastructure, construction of cover structures, use of improved varieties

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural development and climate adaptation
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government ministries, international development organizations, NGOs, research institutions, and private sector actors in agriculture

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Four agro-climatic zones: Northern Zone, Central Coastal Zone, Central Inland Zone, and Southern Zone, with diverse production systems from subsistence to commercial export-oriented agriculture

how_it_works.approach_summary: The CSA approach systematically identifies locally effective practices, diagnoses barriers to adoption, evaluates strategies to overcome barriers, and ensures institutional and financial enablers are in place. It uses a participatory, multi-phase prioritization process involving workshops, interviews, surveys, and focus group discussions.
key_steps: Systematic identification of technically effective, financially profitable, and environmentally sustainable CSA practices
Careful diagnosis of barriers to adoption of those practices
Evaluation of strategies to overcome the barriers
Assessment of climate smartness across productivity, adaptation, and mitigation dimensions
Participatory prioritization process with diverse national actors
Development of best-bet CSA portfolios
Ensuring presence of institutional and financial enablers
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Not specified
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climate projections, agricultural production data, stakeholder consultations, economic analysis of CSA practices, institutional mapping

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Climate modeling capabilities, agricultural impact assessment tools, economic analysis capacity
what_youll_need.human_resources: Agricultural experts, climate specialists, economists, policy analysts, extension agents
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Coordination mechanism among government ministries, research institutions, NGOs, and international partners
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Belize's Third National Communication highlighted that approximately US$ 13 million are required for agricultural adaptation measures

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Identification of best-bet CSA practices, institutional mapping, policy recommendations, financing strategies
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Sustainably increase agricultural productivity and farmers' incomes, adapt and build resilience to climate change, and reduce/remove greenhouse gas emissions
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: CSA practices show positive impacts across multiple dimensions: yield increases, income improvements, water and soil conservation, risk reduction, and GHG mitigation

practical_examples.description: Ya'axché Conservation Trust works with 31 farmers from the Trio Farmers Cacao Growers Association implementing cacao agroforestry and Inga alley cropping on 18% of concession land.
practical_examples.key_result: Projections show more than 10,000 pounds of cacao production by 2019, with goal of generating 75% of participating farmers' income

10568-100326.limitations_and_considerations: Factors of potential policy relevance were not taken into account during prioritization. Data were not available for systematic assessment of gendered impacts. Results reflect judgments of participants in prioritization process. More widespread adoption has been hindered by lack of information, technical knowledge, and resources for initial investment costs.
key_takeaways: Agriculture in Belize is highly vulnerable to climate change, with projected temperature increases of 1.3°C by 2030s and rainfall decreases of 7-10%
Many promising CSA practices already exist and are being used by farmers, including cover structures, drip irrigation, agroforestry, and improved varieties
Mainstreaming CSA requires systematic identification of effective practices, diagnosis of adoption barriers, and ensuring institutional and financial enablers
Multiple institutions are working on CSA but few address all three pillars (productivity, adaptation, mitigation) simultaneously
Additional financing estimated at US$ 13 million is needed for agricultural adaptation, requiring both public and private sector engagement

source_and_citation.full_citation: International Center for Tropical Agriculture; World Bank (2018). Climate-Smart Agriculture in Belize. International Center for Tropical Agriculture; World Bank.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT) and World Bank

10568-100338.overview: This is an evaluation report of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT model for Colombia, which is an animal-level software tool for estimating ruminant methane emissions and livestock productivity. The evaluation uses outcome harvesting methodology to assess how the adapted model has contributed to improving livestock sector knowledge, enhancing productivity through better feed practices, and supporting Colombia's climate change mitigation commitments.

resource_type.type_notes: This is an evaluation report documenting outcomes of a research project, not a framework as indicated in metadata

classification.development_stage_evidence: The modeled and measured results show a good correlation, with an R2 of 0.7... CIAT's Livestock-Plus Program calibrated and validated RUMINANT to adjust it to the types of livestock, food and nutritional qualities of Colombia

who_its_for.primary_audience: Policy makers, technical assistance groups, producer associations, researchers, and livestock sector stakeholders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies (IDEAM, Ministry of Agriculture), livestock federations (FEDEGAN), research institutions, and sustainable livestock organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical livestock systems with forage-based production

how_it_works.approach_summary: RUMINANT is calibrated and validated for Colombian conditions by comparing model predictions with field measurements using polytunnels and laboratory analysis. The tool takes diet quality as input and predicts animal intake, nutrition, production, and methane emissions.
key_steps: Calibrate RUMINANT model for Colombian livestock types and feed qualities
Validate model through field measurements using polytunnels and laboratory analysis
Train stakeholders on model use through workshops and capacity building
Apply model to develop mitigation scenarios and policy commitments
Disseminate results through bulletins, websites, and training programs
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 2014-2018 implementation period based on outcome timeline
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Diet quality data, livestock types, forage nutritional qualities, and local production system characteristics

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: RUMINANT software, laboratory facilities for validation, polytunnel equipment for field measurements
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained technicians, researchers, and specialists familiar with livestock nutrition and greenhouse gas modeling
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with livestock organizations, government agencies, and research institutions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but described as a lower cost option compared to direct field methane measurements

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Calibrated and validated RUMINANT model for Colombian conditions, training materials, policy scenarios, and technical bulletins
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved livestock productivity through better nutrition, reduced methane emissions, and enhanced climate change mitigation strategies
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: R2 correlation of 0.7 between modeled and measured results; 18 documented outcomes including policy adoption, capacity building, and productivity improvements

practical_examples.description: FEDEGAN used RUMINANT to strengthen its sustainable livestock strategy covering 94,000 ha and 1,600 producers
practical_examples.key_result: Improved knowledge sharing about enteric fermentation and sustainable production practices among livestock producers

practical_examples.description: IDEAM integrated RUMINANT into national greenhouse gas inventories and NAMA development
practical_examples.key_result: More precise mitigation commitments and reduced uncertainty in enteric fermentation calculations

practical_examples.description: Multiple universities integrated RUMINANT into master's degree programs and research
practical_examples.key_result: Enhanced technical capacity and validation of the tool across different territories and species

10568-100338.limitations_and_considerations: Recommendations include updating the user interface, integrating data visualization modules with geo-referencing, assessing compatibility with other data sources, and developing a mobile application for producers. The exact numbers of affected farm operations are difficult to estimate.
key_takeaways: RUMINANT provides a cost-effective alternative to expensive field measurements for estimating livestock methane emissions
The tool successfully contributed to Colombia's climate policy development, including NDC targets and livestock NAMA
Capacity building and knowledge sharing were critical for adoption across government agencies, producer organizations, and academic institutions
The model demonstrated strong correlation (R2=0.7) between predicted and measured results, providing confidence for policy applications
Integration with existing livestock programs enhanced both productivity and sustainability outcomes

source_and_citation.full_citation: Camacho, Kemly (2019). An evaluation of the outcomes of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT Model for Colombia. CIAT.

10568-100338._title_meta: An evaluation of the outcomes of the calibration and validation of the RUMINANT Model for Colombia

10568-100343.overview: This resource documents a South-South collaboration workshop conducted by CCAFS in 2019 to build capacity on weather index insurance across multiple regions. The workshop addressed the need for climate risk management tools in agriculture by facilitating knowledge exchange and hands-on training on developing weather index insurance products for smallholder farmers.

resource_type.type_notes: Workshop proceedings that include detailed training content, exercises, and capacity building activities

classification.development_stage_evidence: Workshop included presentations on 'evolution and status of index insurance in Senegal' and case studies from Honduras and Nicaragua showing implemented projects

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, researchers, and policymakers working on climate risk management and agricultural insurance
who_its_for.organizational_context: CGIAR centers, research institutions, insurance companies, government agencies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Smallholder farming systems, both rainfed and irrigated

how_it_works.approach_summary: The workshop used hands-on Excel-based exercises to teach participants how to develop weather triggers and calculate premiums for index insurance products. It combined technical training with cross-regional knowledge sharing and collaborative planning.
key_steps: Introduction to crop-weather relationships and critical growth phases
Hands-on trigger design using Excel-based exercises
Premium estimation and ratemaking calculations
Optimization techniques for improving weather triggers
Regional group discussions on developing weather trigger atlas
Planning for Crop-loss Assessment Monitor (CAM) tool development
Development of regional work plans for scaling insurance products
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 4-day intensive workshop (February 25-28, 2019)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Historical weather data, crop yield data, satellite data, station weather data for rainfall and temperature

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Excel software for hands-on exercises, access to weather and crop data, Climate Data Tool (CDT) for satellite data processing
what_youll_need.human_resources: Technical experts in weather index insurance, crop modeling specialists, regional coordinators
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with insurance companies, meteorological agencies, research institutions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Trained participants capable of developing weather index insurance products, regional work plans for CAM tool development, weather trigger and premium rate atlas for different regions
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced climate risk management for smallholder farmers through improved access to weather index insurance products
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Workshop received high ratings with average effectiveness score of 4+ out of 5 from participants

practical_examples.description: Evolution and implementation of index insurance products with involvement of CNAAS and Planet Guarantee
practical_examples.key_result: Established insurance products with active stakeholder engagement

practical_examples.description: Participatory approach combining remote sensing and participatory rural appraisals for index insurance development
practical_examples.key_result: Community-engaged insurance product development

practical_examples.description: Index insurance developed using satellite weather generator MarkSIM
practical_examples.key_result: Satellite-based weather data integration for insurance products

practical_examples.description: Use of satellite weather data for developing index insurance with focus on salinity and excessive rainfall perils
practical_examples.key_result: Region-specific peril coverage addressing local climate challenges

10568-100343.limitations_and_considerations: Key challenges include data availability issues (station weather and crop production data not available in some regions), need for external support in trigger design and ratemaking, and requirements for stakeholder engagement and regulatory support for scaling insurance products.
key_takeaways: South-South collaboration effectively facilitates knowledge transfer on weather index insurance across regions
Hands-on Excel-based training provides practical skills for developing weather triggers and premium calculations
Regional adaptation is crucial as different areas face distinct perils (drought, floods, salinity, pests) and have varying data availability
Hybrid insurance products combining multiple data sources and models show promise for improving accuracy and coverage
Successful scaling requires strong partnerships with insurance companies, meteorological agencies, and government institutions

source_and_citation.full_citation: Vyas, Shalika; Shirsath, Paresh Bhaskar; Aggarwal, Pramod K. (2019). South-South Collaboration in CCAFS for developing capacity on weather index insurance. CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS).
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CGIAR Research Program on Climate Change, Agriculture and Food Security (CCAFS)

10568-100345.overview: This resource documents a comprehensive evaluation framework for assessing diverse solar irrigation systems across India to identify scalable models. It addresses the challenge of scaling solar irrigation technologies despite heavy subsidies, by systematically reviewing existing pilots and developing indicators for scalability, sustainability, inclusion and climate-smart agriculture.

resource_type.type_notes: Describes a systematic evaluation framework with indicator-based assessment methodology

classification.development_stage_evidence: Several pilots and models on solar irrigation systems have been rolled out in the country

who_its_for.primary_audience: Practitioners, researchers, policy makers working on agricultural water management and climate adaptation
who_its_for.organizational_context: Development organizations, research institutions, government agencies, private sector

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Areas with groundwater irrigation potential and solar energy viability

how_it_works.approach_summary: The framework uses a three-pronged approach: developing a compendium of solar irrigation pilots, conducting systematic review using indicator-based evaluation, and promoting suitable models through stakeholder engagement.
key_steps: Develop compendium of major solar irrigation pilot projects across India
Conduct representative sample surveys in states with significant solar irrigation installations
Document problems, socio-economic impacts, and implementation strategies
Develop indicator-based framework integrating scalability, sustainability, inclusion and climate-smart agriculture
Analyze each pilot initiative for strategies that worked in specific contexts
Conduct workshops and stakeholder consultations for feedback and knowledge sharing
Advocate strategies to promote suitable solar irrigation models
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Sample surveys, pilot project documentation, bio-physical and socio-economic data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Indicator-based evaluation framework, survey methodology
what_youll_need.human_resources: Researchers, field surveyors, stakeholder engagement specialists
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Collaboration among researchers, local partners, private sector and policy makers

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Compilation of working solar irrigation models, evaluation guide for scalability, knowledge products on implementation practices
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Facilitate scaling of efficient solar irrigation models, support climate adaptation and mitigation, contribute to India's green energy targets
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Solar powered irrigation pumps can contribute 38% of India's green energy target

practical_examples.location: Multiple states in India (Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, Chhattisgarh, Bihar, Rajasthan, Gujarat)
practical_examples.description: Systematic documentation and evaluation of solar irrigation pilots across six major states
practical_examples.key_result: Representative sample covering significant portion of total solar irrigation systems installed in India

10568-100348.overview: This academic paper presents a high-throughput method for estimating above-ground biomass in rice crops using UAV-mounted multispectral cameras and vegetation indices. The approach combines seven vegetation indices through multivariable regressions calibrated for different rice growth stages (vegetative, reproductive, ripening) and validated using small 1-meter sampling areas.

resource_type.type_notes: Published research article in Journal of Intelligent & Robotic Systems presenting novel methodology and experimental results

classification.development_stage_evidence: Comprehensive experimental tests have been carried out over two different rice varieties under upland and lowland rice production systems... Results show that the proposed approach is able to estimate the biomass of large areas of the crop with an average correlation of 0.76

who_its_for.primary_audience: Agricultural researchers, precision agriculture specialists, crop monitoring technicians
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural technology companies, precision agriculture service providers

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Both upland (dry soil) and lowland (flooded) rice production systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses UAV-mounted multispectral cameras to capture near-infrared imagery of rice crops, processes images through clustering algorithms to identify plant areas, calculates seven vegetation indices, and applies multivariable regression models calibrated for specific growth stages to estimate biomass.
key_steps: Capture multispectral NIR images using UAV at 12m altitude
Apply image pre-processing including feature extraction and k-means clustering
Segment crop areas using either column or circle segmentation methods
Calculate seven vegetation indices (NDVI, GNDVI, SR, CTVI, SAVI, DVI, MSAVI)
Apply stage-specific multivariable regression models
Generate biomass estimates with GPS geo-referencing
Validate against ground-truth destructive sampling measurements
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: 7 months of field measurements across three crop growth stages
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Multispectral imagery, GPS/IMU data, ground-truth biomass measurements from 1-meter sampling areas

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: UAV platform, Tetracam ADC-lite multispectral camera, GPS/IMU sensors, image processing software with custom GUI

what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Access to experimental rice fields, laboratory facilities for biomass drying and weighing
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Non-destructive, high-throughput monitoring of rice biomass for precision agriculture and breeding programs
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Average correlation of 0.76 compared with traditional manual destructive method across 18,000 processed images

practical_examples.description: Testing on Linea 23 rice variety adapted to dry soil conditions with 95-day phenological cycle
practical_examples.key_result: Successfully estimated biomass across vegetative, reproductive, and ripening stages

practical_examples.description: Testing on IR64 rice variety adapted to flooded paddies with 110-day phenological cycle
practical_examples.key_result: Achieved biomass correlations above 0.75 using circle segmentation method

10568-100348.limitations_and_considerations: Weather conditions can affect image quality, especially in lowland crops where water reflectance changes image appearance. Method requires minimum 10 images per parcel for reliable estimation.
key_takeaways: First work to use 1-meter sampling areas for calibrating NIR image-based biomass estimation in rice
Circle segmentation method using GPS data achieves higher accuracy than column segmentation
Different vegetation indices perform better at different growth stages, requiring stage-specific calibration
Multivariable regression outperforms neural networks for ripening stage estimation
Method shows potential for breeding programs where plot sizes are limited to 1 linear meter

10568-100360.overview: This MSc thesis investigates the effects of phosphorus fertilizer and rhizobium inoculation on grain and haulm (crop residue) yield and nutritional quality of four grain legumes (faba bean, chickpea, haricot bean, and soybean) in Ethiopia's mixed crop-livestock production systems. The research addresses the dual challenge of improving both grain production for human consumption and haulm quality for livestock feed in smallholder farming systems.

key_steps: Establish field experiments across N2-Africa project target districts
Apply four fertilizer treatments to grain legume plots
Harvest crops and collect grain and haulm samples
Conduct laboratory analysis for nutritional quality parameters
Administer household surveys on feed use practices
Analyze data using statistical methods
Evaluate treatment effects on yield and quality

expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Significant improvements in grain and haulm yield for most crops, with combined treatments showing highest mean values. Faba bean haulm CP content increased by 26.26% with combined treatment.

Improvement in haulm crude protein content and digestibility can enhance livestock nutrition in mixed crop-livestock systems

10568-100495.overview: This document presents a multi-criteria framework for identifying and prioritizing climate-smart agriculture (CSA) practices for Belize. The CSA Prioritization Framework helps policymakers target scarce investment resources more effectively by evaluating CSA practices across technical, economic, and sociocultural factors to identify promising options for adoption and scaling.

resource_type.type_notes: Document describes a multi-criteria decision-support framework with methodology and application results

classification.development_stage_evidence: The Belize CSA Prioritization Framework was tested using a participatory process that elicited experiences from national actors through workshops, interviews, surveys, and focus group discussions

who_its_for.primary_audience: Policymakers, program administrators, development agencies, and private firms including finance organizations
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, international development organizations, agricultural research institutions, private sector firms

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Low-lying coastal nations vulnerable to climate change, subtropical climate with adequate rainfall

how_it_works.approach_summary: The framework uses a multi-phase participatory exercise to identify and prioritize CSA practices through stakeholder engagement, literature review, and multi-criteria evaluation. It assesses practices across economic criteria (NPV, IRR, payback period) and implementation factors to create portfolios of promising CSA options.
key_steps: Conduct comprehensive literature review and stakeholder consultation
Identify long list of potential CSA practices through participatory workshops
Apply multi-criteria evaluation framework to assess practices
Analyze economic criteria including costs, benefits, and returns
Evaluate barriers and opportunities for adoption
Filter to short list of promising practices by cost category
Develop implementation strategies and financing approaches
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-phase process with short-term (1-3 years), medium-term (4-6 years), and long-term (>6 years) implementation timelines
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Stakeholder input through workshops and surveys, economic data on costs and benefits, technical information on CSA practices, policy and institutional context analysis

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Multi-criteria decision analysis capabilities, economic assessment tools, participatory workshop facilitation
what_youll_need.human_resources: Technical experts in agriculture and climate change, facilitators for stakeholder engagement, economic analysts
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Government partnership, stakeholder coordination mechanisms, research institution collaboration
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Estimated total cost of approximately BZ$ 27 million for full implementation of identified actions, with 97% needed for infrastructure and equipment

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Prioritized list of CSA practices categorized by cost (low, medium, high), economic analysis of practices including NPV and IRR, identification of barriers and enabling factors
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: More effective targeting of CSA investments, increased adoption of climate-smart practices, enhanced agricultural productivity and climate resilience
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Framework successfully identified promising practices including cover structures, drip irrigation, water harvesting, crop rotation, and agroforestry systems with positive economic returns

practical_examples.description: Application identified low-cost practices like crop rotation of corn and beans with investment costs of US$ 225 per acre and positive NPV of US$ 618
practical_examples.key_result: Short payback period of 1 year making practices attractive for farmer adoption

practical_examples.description: Medium-cost practice of rearing Brangus cattle on improved pastures requiring initial investment of US$ 1,852.50
practical_examples.key_result: Generated attractive returns with NPV of US$ 3,646 and IRR of 52%

practical_examples.description: High-cost practice of cover structures for vegetable production requiring US$ 1,555 initial investment
practical_examples.key_result: Generated very attractive NPV of US$ 6,266.50 despite high upfront costs

10568-100495.limitations_and_considerations: Results reflect subjective judgments of particular stakeholder groups and should be considered indicative. Adoption barriers include lack of information and technical knowledge, land tenure insecurity, infrastructure deficiencies, and economic constraints. High-cost practices require significant enabling factors including access to financing and supportive policy environments.
key_takeaways: Multi-criteria frameworks can effectively prioritize CSA investments by considering technical, economic, and sociocultural factors
CSA practices can be categorized by implementation cost, with different financing and policy approaches needed for each category
Low-cost practices often have short payback periods but may face adoption barriers unrelated to profitability
Medium and high-cost practices require coordinated public-private financing approaches and enabling policy environments
The Maximizing Finance for Development approach provides a systematic framework for leveraging private sector resources in CSA investments

10568-100512.overview: This is a gender assessment study that examines local understanding and indicators of empowerment among smallholder poultry farmers participating in the African Chicken Genetic Gains (ACGG) project in Nigeria. The study addresses the need to understand context-specific meanings of empowerment to inform effective interventions and measurement approaches for women's empowerment in agricultural development projects.

Conduct gender-specific focus group discussions
Use pilot ACGG empowerment tool as FGD guide
Transcribe and analyze data using ATLAS.ti qualitative analysis
Code quotations and calculate frequencies by gender
Analyze local understanding and empowerment indicators
Compare gender perspectives across study areas

expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Inform selection of indicators to measure women's and men's empowerment and identify interventions to close gender gaps in agricultural development projects
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: The study revealed significant gender differences in empowerment conceptualization, with women focusing on service provision and capacity building while men emphasized personal development and authority

practical_examples.description: Gender-responsive credit schemes and training programs with inclusive committees promoting joint ownership of productive assets
practical_examples.key_result: Increased women's participation in livestock sector training and financial services access

practical_examples.description: Dairy women groups in Arusha and Kilimanjaro working across production, processing and marketing nodes
practical_examples.key_result: Collective problem-solving, capacity building, and establishment of school milk feeding programs

10568-100516.limitations_and_considerations: The training requires significant institutional commitment and follow-up support. Cultural and social barriers may limit immediate application of gender transformative approaches. Success depends on ongoing political and institutional support at country level. Implementation may face resistance from staff or communities not ready for gender-focused interventions.
key_takeaways: Gender analysis is critical for effective livestock development as women comprise 2/3 of poor livestock keepers but face systematic barriers to resources and decision-making
Interactive, practical training approaches using familiar concepts like value chains and project cycles are most effective for building gender analysis capacity
Country-specific action plans and follow-up support are essential for translating training into actual practice and policy changes
Successful gender integration requires both technical knowledge and institutional commitment at multiple levels
Evidence-based approaches and sex-disaggregated data collection are fundamental for understanding and addressing gender gaps in livestock development

10568-100524.overview: A comprehensive training module designed to educate farmers in the Ethiopian highlands about anthrax transmission, clinical signs, and prevention measures. The module addresses farmers' limited knowledge about this zoonotic disease that affects livestock and can be transmitted to humans, providing practical guidance for disease prevention and control.

resource_type.type_notes: Clearly structured as a learning module with sessions, objectives, activities, and assessment methods

classification.development_stage_evidence: Module is designed for men and women farmers in the highlands of Ethiopia with specific cultural considerations and local context integration

who_its_for.primary_audience: Men and women farmers in Ethiopian highlands
who_its_for.organizational_context: Community-based training programs, agricultural extension services, livestock development organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Highland farming systems with small ruminant production

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses active and collaborative learning methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences. Employs visualizations, group activities, practical demonstrations, video clips, role-play and reflective activities to enhance learning and knowledge application.
key_steps: Session 1: Learn about anthrax importance and causes
Session 2: Understand anthrax transmission pathways
Session 3: Identify clinical signs in animals and humans
Session 4: Apply prevention and control measures
Develop action plans for implementation
Conduct follow-up assessment and monitoring

how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Pre-training assessment of farmers' knowledge, attitudes and skills

what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Community centers for training delivery
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Farmers able to describe anthrax causes and transmission, identify clinical signs, apply control measures, avoid consuming infected meat, and regularly vaccinate animals
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Prevent transmission of anthrax to humans and reduce livestock losses due to anthrax infection
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Pre- and post-training assessments measure knowledge gains; follow-up interviews 3-6 months after training evaluate retention and application

practical_examples.description: Training designed specifically for highland farming communities with small ruminant production systems
practical_examples.key_result: Addresses farmers' limited knowledge about anthrax cause, transmission pathways, and prevention measures

10568-100524.limitations_and_considerations: Requires cultural sensitivity regarding burial practices and traditional customs. Incineration of dead animals may be culturally prohibited in some contexts. Mixed training approach recommended to include both male and female farmers for collaborative learning.
key_takeaways: Anthrax is a serious zoonotic disease caused by spore-forming bacteria that can persist in soil for decades
Clinical signs include sudden death, high temperature, blood discharge from body openings, and loss of appetite in animals
Prevention requires annual vaccination, proper carcass disposal, protective equipment use, and immediate reporting to veterinary officers
Training uses participatory methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences
Assessment includes pre/post training evaluation and follow-up monitoring for knowledge retention and application

10568-100525.overview: A comprehensive training module designed for farmers in the Ethiopian highlands to learn about coenurosis, a parasitic disease affecting sheep and goats caused by dog tapeworms. The module covers transmission pathways, clinical signs, prevention and control measures, and practical skills like dog fecal sample collection to help farmers protect their livestock and improve animal health.

resource_type.type_notes: Explicitly described as a learning module with structured sessions, learning objectives, and training activities

classification.development_stage_evidence: The module is designed for trainers who are new to their role but have significant experience with the concepts, suggesting it has been developed based on field experience

who_its_for.primary_audience: Women and men farmers in the highlands of Ethiopia, community animal health workers, and training facilitators
who_its_for.organizational_context: Rural farming communities, livestock development organizations, veterinary extension services

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Highland farming systems with sheep and goat production

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses active and collaborative learning methods building on farmers' existing knowledge and experiences. Combines theoretical sessions with practical demonstrations and group activities to teach about coenurosis transmission, prevention, and control.
key_steps: Introduction to coenurosis and its causes
Learning transmission pathways of the disease
Identifying clinical signs in affected animals
Understanding prevention and control measures
Practicing dog fecal sample collection and submission
Developing community action plans
Post-training evaluation and follow-up
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: One full day training or two half-day sessions
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Pre- and post-training assessments, participant experiences with livestock health issues

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Posters, leaflets, illustrations, PowerPoint presentations, sample collection materials, formalin containers
what_youll_need.human_resources: Training facilitators with experience in livestock health concepts, community leaders for opening ceremonies
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Community centers for training delivery, access to laboratory facilities for fecal sample analysis
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Materials for sample collection (gloves, containers, labels), deworming drugs like Praziquantel

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Farmers able to explain coenurosis transmission, identify clinical signs, implement prevention measures, collect dog fecal samples, and follow community deworming schedules
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved livestock and dog health, reduced economic losses from coenurosis, prevention of zoonotic disease transmission to humans
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Pre- and post-training assessments measure knowledge gains, with follow-up interviews 3-6 months later to evaluate retention and application

practical_examples.description: Training designed specifically for highland farming communities where farmers have limited knowledge about coenurosis transmission and control
practical_examples.key_result: Addresses significant livestock losses and improves understanding of the relationship between dog and livestock health

10568-100525.limitations_and_considerations: The module is specifically designed for Ethiopian highland contexts and may need adaptation for other regions. Success depends on community participation in collective dog deworming and proper disposal of infected animal heads. Access to deworming drugs and laboratory facilities for fecal analysis may be limiting factors.
key_takeaways: Coenurosis is caused by dog tapeworms and can be prevented through proper management of dogs and infected livestock
Community-based approaches including collective dog deworming every three months are essential for effective control
Farmers must avoid feeding sheep and goat heads to dogs and properly dispose of infected carcasses by burning or burying
The disease has both economic impacts on livestock and public health implications through related zoonotic infections
Practical training combining theory with hands-on activities like fecal sample collection improves learning outcomes

10568-100529.overview: This research analyzes socio-economic factors that influence farmers' adoption of soil carbon enhancing land management practices in Ethiopia and Kenya. It combines a structured literature review of 144 studies with analysis of household data to identify constraints and facilitators for sustainable land management practices that improve soil carbon sequestration.

resource_type.type_notes: Presentation format but contains comprehensive research methodology, literature review, and analytical results typical of academic research

classification.development_stage_evidence: Analysis of WOCAT and CIAT-best bet practices database with household clustering analysis and regression results from Ethiopia and Kenya

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers working on sustainable land management and soil carbon enhancement
who_its_for.organizational_context: CGIAR centers, development organizations, government agencies, research institutions

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Areas where farming is primary activity and land degradation through soil and nutrient loss is common

how_it_works.approach_summary: Combines structured literature review of adoption studies with quantitative analysis of household data using agglomerative hierarchical clustering to identify farmer typologies and factors affecting adoption decisions.
key_steps: Structured literature review of publications on adoption of agricultural and SLM practices
Creation of database containing socio-economic factors from literature
Analysis of WOCAT and CIAT-best bet practices household data
Agglomerative hierarchical clustering to group households into three homogenous groups
Regression analysis to identify determinants of adoption
Synthesis of findings across countries and practice types
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Household survey data on socio-economic characteristics, land management practices, and adoption decisions

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Statistical software for clustering analysis and regression modeling (probit, logit, tobit models)
what_youll_need.human_resources: Researchers with expertise in agricultural economics and statistical analysis
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Access to household survey databases and literature review resources

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Identification of key socio-economic factors that constrain or facilitate adoption of soil carbon enhancing practices
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved policy and strategy formulation for promoting sustainable land management practices that enhance soil carbon sequestration
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Analysis of 144 studies (90 Ethiopia, 54 Kenya) with average sample sizes of 350-948 households showing varied effects of factors like age, gender, income, education, and land tenure on adoption

practical_examples.description: Analysis of adoption patterns for minimum tillage, intercropping, tree planting, farmyard manure, and soil bunds among 350 average household sample
practical_examples.key_result: 86% of households are extensive land users with joint male-female decision making and positive short-run benefit-cost evaluation

practical_examples.location: Kenya (Coast, Western, Nyanza, Central, Eastern and Rift Valley)
practical_examples.description: Study of terracing, minimum tillage, intercropping, and agroforestry adoption among 948 average household sample
practical_examples.key_result: 68% of farmers have off-farm income <$100, with 70% and 92% weighing benefits vs costs as positive in short and long run respectively

10568-100529.limitations_and_considerations: Adoption effects vary significantly by technology type and farming context. External factors affect decisions indirectly through net returns. Effectiveness depends on institutional capacity to provide technical support. Most farmers are sensitive to net returns on labor and financial investment.
key_takeaways: Socio-economic factors have varied influence on adoption depending on the nature of the soil carbon enhancing technology
Factors affecting farmer incentive to invest are related to conditions that affect net returns to labor or financial investment
Age, gender, income, education, extension services, and land tenure security show consistent patterns across both countries
Policy frameworks need to incorporate adoption constraints and enablers to facilitate increased uptake
Need for stable land tenure systems and enabling conditions to enhance adoption of soil carbon enhancing practices

10568-100534.overview: CTA's 2018 Annual Report documents the organization's work accelerating agricultural transformation across ACP countries through three main intervention areas: promoting youth entrepreneurship and employment, increasing productivity and profitability through digitalization, and building resilience to climate change. The report showcases how digital technologies, climate-smart practices, and targeted support for women and youth are transforming agricultural value chains and improving food security.

resource_type.type_notes: Annual report documenting organizational activities and outcomes

classification.development_stage_evidence: Over 1 million farmers have benefited from services of youth start-ups involved in CTA's annual Pitch AgriHack competitions

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Development organizations, government agencies, agricultural research institutions, private sector companies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Diverse contexts including pastoralist systems, smallholder farming, island agriculture

how_it_works.approach_summary: CTA implements integrated approaches combining digital technologies, climate-smart practices, and targeted support for youth and women entrepreneurs across agricultural value chains. Projects focus on capacity building, technology adoption, and market linkages.
key_steps: Digital profiling of farmers and agricultural stakeholders
Training and capacity building for youth entrepreneurs and women
Technology deployment (mobile apps, drones, weather information systems)
Market linkage facilitation and value chain development
Climate-smart agriculture promotion including insurance schemes
Partnership building between public and private sectors
Monitoring and evaluation of outcomes

what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships between government, private sector, and development organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Projects range from thousands to millions of euros, with specific examples like €230,000 loan fund in Uganda

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Digital profiling of 85,000+ farmers, training of 4,166 individuals in open data, 10,827 pastoralists purchasing livestock insurance
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased agricultural productivity (25-90% yield increases), improved incomes (€25-850 additional per farmer annually), enhanced climate resilience
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Soybean yields increased by 60% and bean yields by 90% in Uganda MUIIS project; over 3,000 coffee farmers saw 25%+ income increases

practical_examples.description: MUIIS project provided digital profiling, agronomic advice, weather alerts and insurance to smallholder farmers via mobile phones, combined with targeted loans.
practical_examples.key_result: Soybean yields increased by 60% and bean yields by 90% among participating farmers

practical_examples.description: Pitch AgriHack 2018 competition focused on women entrepreneurs, with 26 finalists (14 women-led) presenting agricultural technology innovations.
practical_examples.key_result: Over 1 million farmers have benefited from services provided by start-ups from these competitions

practical_examples.description: CLI-MARK project provided livestock insurance, weather information, and market linkages to 100,000 pastoralists to build climate resilience.
practical_examples.key_result: Number of pastoralists with livestock insurance increased from 707 to 2,942 in Ethiopia

10568-100534.limitations_and_considerations: Challenges include farmer willingness to pay for services (initially only 379 farmers subscribed to MUIIS), regulatory uncertainties for drone operations, and need for sustained partnerships beyond project timelines. key_takeaways: Digital technologies combined with traditional agricultural services can dramatically increase productivity and farmer incomes
Youth and women entrepreneurs are key drivers of agricultural transformation when provided with appropriate training and support
Climate-smart approaches including insurance schemes are essential for building resilience in vulnerable farming communities
Strong partnerships between government, private sector, and development organizations are critical for scaling impact
Experience capitalization and learning approaches help ensure project sustainability and knowledge transfer

10568-100540.overview: A comprehensive monitoring framework and ICT-based data collection tool for assessing Climate-Smart Agriculture (CSA) adoption and impacts across Climate-Smart Villages (CSV). The framework includes 44 standard indicators covering productivity, adaptation, and mitigation pillars, implemented through a mobile app to track CSA practices and their effects on farming communities.

resource_type.type_notes: Described as a monitoring framework with an ICT-based data collection instrument (Smart App) for systematic deployment across CSV networks

classification.development_stage_evidence: Between February and December 2018 it was implemented by locally trained enumerators across 8 CSV sites in LAM, EA and SA, interviewing over 2,300 farmers

who_its_for.primary_audience: CCAFS researchers, CSV coordinators, local enumerators, and development practitioners working on climate-smart agriculture monitoring
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, development organizations, and agricultural extension services implementing CSV programs

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Diverse agricultural systems across tropical and subtropical regions

how_it_works.approach_summary: Uses a mobile app (GeoFarmer Smart App) to collect standardized data on CSA adoption and impacts through structured household interviews. Data is collected across 9 thematic modules covering demographics, climate shocks, livelihood security, food security, and CSA options.
key_steps: Set up surveys and tailor Smart App for each CSV site
Conduct 1-week regional and local training for coordinators and enumerators
Implement sampling strategy targeting baseline households and CSA beneficiaries
Conduct household interviews with head of household and opposite-sex partner
Complete farm performance calculator modules with sub-sample of adopting farmers
Analyze data using R scripts to calculate 44 standard indicators
Generate automated reports and visualizations through GeoFarmer dashboard
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Annual monitoring cycle with data collection between February-December 2018
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Household demographic data, CSA practice adoption status, perceived effects on livelihoods, farm performance data, gender-disaggregated responses

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Tablets or cellphones with GeoFarmer Smart App installed, R software for data analysis
what_youll_need.human_resources: Regional CCAFS coordinators, local enumerators, local research partners as supervisors, trained data collectors
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with CSV sites, access to baseline household data, coordination with local agricultural extension services
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: 1-week training programs per region, equipment for data collection, personnel costs for enumerators and supervisors

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Standardized data on CSA adoption rates, perceived effects on productivity/income/food security, gender-disaggregated impacts, farm-level performance metrics
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Evidence-based understanding of CSA effectiveness, identification of adoption barriers and enablers, support for scaling up promising CSA options
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully collected data from 2,337 farmers across 8 CSV sites, with high reported positive effects (60-95%) of top CSA practices on production, income, and climate vulnerability reduction

practical_examples.description: Monitored 189 households implementing crop rotation, water harvesting, and organic fertilizer practices
practical_examples.key_result: 94% of farmers reported positive effects of improved varieties on production, 95% reported positive effects on climate vulnerability reduction

practical_examples.description: Largest implementation covering 343 households with focus on improved varieties and intercropping
practical_examples.key_result: 83% of farmers reported positive production effects from improved varieties, 89% reported improved food access from intercropping

practical_examples.description: Focused on organic fertilizer and rainwater harvesting with 166 households
practical_examples.key_result: 100% of farmers reported improved varieties decreased climate vulnerability, 87% reported improved food access

10568-100540.limitations_and_considerations: Data analysis was still in progress at time of report, with only preliminary results available for perceived effects. The framework relies on farmer perceptions rather than measured outcomes. Implementation requires significant coordination and training across multiple sites and regions.
key_takeaways: The framework successfully standardizes CSA monitoring across diverse geographical and agricultural contexts using 44 robust indicators
Mobile app-based data collection enables real-time monitoring and systematic deployment across global CSV networks
Top adopted CSA practices (improved varieties, intercropping, organic fertilizer) show consistently high positive effects on production, income, and climate resilience
Gender-disaggregated data collection reveals differential adoption patterns and impacts between male and female-headed households
The system provides critical evidence for evaluating CSA efficacy and scaling up locally relevant practices
