10568-100541.overview: This handbook explores how national climate change policies in the Philippines are translated into local actions in the agriculture sector, with a focus on rice and irrigation systems. It describes the institutional setting, implementation dynamics, and bottlenecks to effective climate change policy implementation at the local level, based on a case study in Central Luzon.

resource_type.type_notes: Handbook providing guidance on climate change policy implementation processes and institutional frameworks

classification.development_stage_evidence: Based on research conducted from June 2016 to January 2017 with key informant interviews and focus group discussions in Central Luzon

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, NGOs, research institutions, and international development organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Rice-based irrigated systems, particularly in Central Luzon

how_it_works.approach_summary: The handbook maps the climate change policy pathway from national to local levels through institutional actors and describes how policies are mainstreamed, implemented, and monitored at different governance levels.
key_steps: National climate change policy formulation
Sectoral mainstreaming through agencies like DA and NIA
Local legitimization through ordinances and development plans
Program planning and execution at local level
Implementation through production, extension, and R&D programs
Monitoring and evaluation of outcomes
Institutional strengthening and capacity building
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Policy implementation is described as having 'long and seemingly interminable time lag from policy issuance at the national level to compliance at the local level'
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Key informant interviews, focus group discussions, secondary data from government agencies

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Understanding of Philippine governance structure and climate change policy framework
what_youll_need.human_resources: Climate change specialists, policy analysts, local government officials, extension workers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Multi-level institutional coordination between national agencies (CCC, DA, NIA), regional offices, and local government units
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Enhanced understanding of climate change policy implementation dynamics, identification of bottlenecks, and intervention recommendations
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: More effective climate change response and mitigation in agriculture through improved policy implementation
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Case study findings from Central Luzon showing varying degrees of policy integration and implementation challenges

practical_examples.description: NIA-UPRIIS integrated alternate wetting and drying (AWD) water-saving technologies in their irrigation development program
practical_examples.key_result: 30,235 farmers (34% of total membership) registered as AWD practitioners or adopters by 2016

practical_examples.description: Some municipalities issued ordinances prohibiting rice straw burning to prevent methane emissions
practical_examples.key_result: Local-level climate change mitigation policy implementation through municipal ordinances

10568-100541.limitations_and_considerations: Key limitations include: slow climate change policy mainstreaming at local level (only 10% of LGUs prepared local climate change action plans), fragmented implementation across agencies, weak monitoring and evaluation systems using output rather than outcome indicators, structural deficiencies in specialized climate change units, and low absorptive capacity of local institutions and farmers.
key_takeaways: Climate change policy implementation in the Philippines follows a complex multi-level pathway with significant time lags between national policy issuance and local compliance
Major bottlenecks include absence of local government ordinances, structural deficiencies in climate change organizations, and weak coordination mechanisms
Implementation occurs primarily through existing agricultural programs rather than dedicated climate change programs, leading to fragmented approaches
Institutional strengthening is needed at local levels, particularly for LGUs, to enhance capacity for policy mainstreaming and program implementation
A more unified, systems-wide approach to climate change program planning and coordination is essential for effective implementation

National legislation including Climate Change Act and agriculture-specific laws

key_takeaways: Philippines has developed a comprehensive hierarchy of climate change policies spanning constitutional provisions to local ordinances
Agriculture sector, particularly rice production, is prioritized in climate change adaptation and mitigation policies

who_its_for.primary_audience: Scientists, extension agents, and development practitioners working with smallholder agricultural settings
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, extension services, development organizations working on landscape management

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Semi-arid areas with annual rainfall of 300-800 mm, hilly landscapes with varying slope gradients

how_it_works.approach_summary: The guidelines provide a systematic approach to selecting and implementing soil and water conservation measures based on landscape characteristics, combining biological, mechanical, and bio-engineering methods tailored to local conditions and farmer needs.
key_steps: Define conservation objectives based on local conditions
Assess slope gradients and lengths using threshold limits (≤5% gradient, ≤20m length)
Select appropriate conservation measures from biological, mechanical, or bio-engineering options
Implement in-situ water harvesting techniques like tied ridging for slopes <7%
Train farmers and extension agents on proper implementation
Monitor and maintain structures throughout growing season
Evaluate effectiveness using farmer scorecards and technical measurements
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Implementation varies by technique; tied ridges require 2-3 maintenance cycles per season for sandy soils, 1 for clay soils
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Climatological data, slope measurements, soil type assessment, rainfall patterns, land use/cover data

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Oxen/donkey ridger/ripper drawn tillage implements, hand tools, measurement equipment for slope and infiltration assessment
what_youll_need.human_resources: 25-35 labor days per hectare for manual tied ridging, trained extension agents, farmer groups
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Water resources users associations, partnerships between public and private sector entities
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Cost-effectiveness emphasized as critical for smallholder adoption; specific costs not provided but noted as potentially prohibitive

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Reduced soil erosion, increased water infiltration, improved soil moisture retention, enhanced crop yields
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced yield stability, household food security, reduced sediment loads in watersheds, improved landscape health
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Tied ridging increased sorghum grain yield by 2-3 fold compared to conventional tillage; areas with grass strips & terraces showed 30% better water yields and 45% better sedimentation retention

practical_examples.description: Training of 80 farmers and extension agents on water quality monitoring and erosion prevention using farmer scorecards for landscape health evaluation
practical_examples.key_result: Order of magnitude increase in runoff for areas without sustainable land management, 40% increase in sediment losses

practical_examples.description: Land degradation assessment and scenario modeling using SWAT hydrological models to identify hotspots and intervention options
practical_examples.key_result: Conversion of 60% tea lands to agriculture would increase sediment loads by 23-27%, while replacing 40% agricultural areas with tea would reduce sediment loads by 17-21%

practical_examples.description: Implementation of forage grass-legume intercrops on terraced slopes for soil and water conservation
practical_examples.key_result: 40-60% lower runoff levels and 30% higher soil moisture storage (25mm over 50cm depth) compared to control areas

10568-100546.limitations_and_considerations: Tied ridging works only on slopes <7%; high labor requirements (25-35 days/ha) without mechanization; inappropriate for well-drained sandy soils; requires visible short-term benefits for farmer adoption; costs can be prohibitive; technology must be coupled with farmer awareness and support; some degree of erosion may be acceptable if complete elimination is unrealistic.
key_takeaways: No universal conservation practices work everywhere - planning requires selecting techniques relevant to local conditions and combining them into workable systems
Threshold limits of ≤5% slope gradient and ≤20m slope length help determine appropriate intervention spacing and prevent significant soil losses
In-situ water harvesting techniques like tied ridging can increase crop yields 2-3 fold in semi-arid areas with 300-800mm annual rainfall
Successful implementation requires cost-effective solutions with visible short-term benefits, farmer training, and ongoing maintenance
Landscape-scale monitoring using farmer scorecards and hydrological modeling helps identify degradation hotspots and evaluate intervention effectiveness

source_and_citation.full_citation: Kizito, Fred (2019). Monitoring and assessment guidelines and options towards land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes. CIAT Technical Brief.

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Some formatting artifacts from tables and figures, but core content is comprehensive and well-structured

10568-100546._title_meta: Monitoring and assessment guidelines and options towards land restoration and water resources management in agricultural landscapes

10568-100548.overview: This is a comprehensive participatory land use and development plan for Chifra Woreda in Ethiopia's Afar region, developed as a pilot program to address rangeland degradation and improve pastoralist livelihoods. The document presents a detailed methodology for conducting woreda-level participatory land use planning in pastoral and agro-pastoral areas, including land capability classification, stakeholder engagement, and implementation strategies.

resource_type.type_notes: Functions as both a planning tool and implementation guide for participatory land use planning in pastoral areas

classification.development_stage_evidence: This Chifra Woreda Participatory Land Use and Development Plan is a Pilot Program, which is the first in its kind in pastoral and agro pastoral areas of Ethiopia

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, and government officials working in pastoral and agro-pastoral areas
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies (particularly Environmental Protection and Land Use Administration), NGOs, research institutes, and donor organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Arid and semi-arid pastoral and agro-pastoral areas

how_it_works.approach_summary: The approach involves establishing multi-disciplinary taskforces to conduct participatory assessments of biophysical and socio-economic conditions, engaging pastoralists and agro-pastoralists in problem identification and solution analysis, and developing land capability classifications to guide rangeland management decisions.
key_steps: Establish multi-discipline taskforce with rangeland, socio-economic, land capability and GIS teams
Conduct stakeholder analysis and recognition of primary and secondary stakeholders
Collect biophysical data (slope, soil, vegetation, water, climate) and socio-economic data through PRA tools
Assess rangeland conditions across traditional rangeland regimes
Conduct problem and constraint identification through group discussions
Perform land capability classification using modified matrix for pastoral systems
Hold validation workshop with communities and stakeholders
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Three years (2017-2019) for pilot implementation, with 60 days of intensive fieldwork for planning phase
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Biophysical data (slope, soil, vegetation, water, climate), socio-economic surveys, traditional knowledge from pastoralists, satellite imagery, and secondary data on population and livestock

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: GIS capabilities, GPS units, field survey instruments, vehicles/motorcycles, office equipment including computers and printers
what_youll_need.human_resources: Multi-disciplinary team including range ecologist, M&E officer, GIS specialist, socio-economic experts, and local translators; estimated 18 taskforce members plus community participants
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Coordination between woreda, regional and federal government offices, particularly Environmental Protection and Land Use Administration Authority
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Total budget of Birr 42,807,457 (approximately $1.5 million USD) for three-year pilot program, with 10% community contribution through labor and local materials

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Eight land capability management units identified covering 151,902 hectares: 120,910 ha for rangeland improvement, 4,813 ha for restricted rehabilitation and wildlife sanctuary, 19,001 ha for irrigated cropland, and 7,169 ha for community ranch
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Rehabilitation of rangelands, sustainable rangeland management, improved livestock productivity, enhanced traditional management systems, and increased household incomes for pastoralists and agro-pastoralists
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Document notes pastoralists acknowledged multiple benefits including reduced mobility, decreased conflicts, improved livestock productivity, and better access to social services, though this is a pilot program with limited implementation evidence

practical_examples.description: First pilot implementation of woreda-level participatory land use planning in pastoral areas, covering 151,902 hectares across 19 kebeles with 109,997 people
practical_examples.key_result: Identified specific management interventions for different land units and established framework for scaling to other pastoral areas

10568-100548.limitations_and_considerations: The approach requires significant technical expertise and coordination across multiple government levels. Success depends on sustained community engagement, adequate rainfall for rangeland improvement activities, and continued political and financial support. The document notes that pastoral settlement/villagization was excluded due to lack of community consensus and need for extensive preparation.
key_takeaways: Participatory land use planning can be successfully adapted for pastoral and agro-pastoral contexts by incorporating traditional rangeland management systems and mobility patterns
Multi-disciplinary taskforces combining technical expertise with local knowledge are essential for developing appropriate land use plans
Land capability classification systems need modification for pastoral areas to account for rangeland productivity rather than just crop production potential
Water harvesting techniques are critical for rangeland improvement in arid environments where rainfall is insufficient for vegetation growth
Community validation and consensus-building are crucial for ensuring land use plans reflect pastoralist priorities and can be successfully implemented

source_and_citation.full_citation: Ministry of Agriculture, Ethiopia (2016). Chifra Woreda participatory land use and development plan, Afar National Regional State. Environmental Protection, Rural Land Use and Administration Authority (EPLUAA).

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Document contains extensive technical detail and local context that may limit direct transferability to other regions without adaptation

10568-100565.overview: This resource presents insights from three African digital agriculture entrepreneurs on building multi-sided business models for digital agribusiness startups. It addresses the challenge of creating sustainable business models in African markets where traditional single-product approaches often fail due to limited market data and customer purchasing power.

resource_type.type_notes: While metadata labels it as 'Tool', the content is clearly a case study sharing experiences and lessons learned from three entrepreneurs

classification.development_stage_evidence: The entrepreneurs share experiences from 'running our own digital start-ups' with specific examples of implemented solutions like UjuziKilimo's soil sensors and SOWIT's drone technology

who_its_for.primary_audience: Digital agriculture entrepreneurs and startup founders
who_its_for.organizational_context: Digital agriculture startups, agtech companies, and development organizations supporting agricultural innovation

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Various contexts including cereal production systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: Entrepreneurs develop multi-sided business models by creating derivative products based on core technology that serve different customer segments, such as farmers and input companies, to diversify revenue streams and explore market opportunities.
key_steps: Develop core technology or service for primary market (farmers)
Identify derivative applications for the same technology
Create individual value propositions for different customer segments
Leverage data from one segment to provide insights for another
Establish strong management and communication strategies
Implement resource allocation processes across market segments
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Market research data, customer feedback, technology platform, and management systems for resource allocation

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Core technology platform that can be adapted for multiple applications (e.g., remote sensing, soil sensors, drones)
what_youll_need.human_resources: Strong management and communication skills, marketing capabilities across different segments
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Disciplined resource allocation processes and clear value proposition management
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Limited marketing budget that must be strategically allocated across segments

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Multiple revenue streams from different customer segments, market insights, and diversified product offerings
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Increased business sustainability, reduced income risk, maximized return on investment, and expanded market exploration
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Examples show successful diversification: UjuziKilimo leveraging farmer data for input companies, SOWIT expanding from agriculture to surveying/mining, N'kalô adding consultancy services

practical_examples.description: UjuziKilimo developed soil sensors for farmers but leveraged the soil analysis data to generate market insights for input enterprises.
practical_examples.key_result: Created dual revenue streams when farmers couldn't always pay but input companies were willing to pay for market insights

practical_examples.description: SOWIT developed drone-based decision support tools for wheat producers while also selling drone technology to surveying, mining, and construction sectors.
practical_examples.key_result: Ensured short-term income through diversification while developing long-term agricultural solutions

practical_examples.description: N'kalô expanded from farmer market advisory services to consultancy projects and exploring advertising revenue from input companies.
practical_examples.key_result: Addressed sustainability challenges when €0.18 monthly farmer subscriptions proved insufficient

10568-100565.limitations_and_considerations: Diversification can confuse investors who prefer simple business models and may muddy customer communication. It requires careful resource allocation and strong management to avoid spreading marketing budgets too thin across segments. Investors often prefer trending technologies over practical solutions.
key_takeaways: African digital agriculture startups need diversification strategies due to limited market data and customer purchasing power
Multi-sided business models can leverage the same core technology to serve different customer segments and create multiple revenue streams
Strong management and communication skills are essential to address investor concerns and maintain clear value propositions
Disciplined resource allocation processes are crucial to avoid confusion and maintain focus across different market segments
Technology should be viewed as a means to solve farmer challenges rather than an end in itself

source_and_citation.full_citation: Chaham, Hamza R.; Bosire, Brian; Ricau, Pierre (2018). Building multi-sided business models for stronger digital agribusiness market development. CTA.
source_and_citation.publishing_organization: CTA (Technical Centre for Agricultural and Rural Cooperation)

10568-100636.overview: This is a comprehensive dairy value chain assessment report for Kenya's Crops and Dairy Market Systems Activity, analyzing the dairy sector in 12 target counties in western and lower eastern Kenya. The report identifies productivity gaps, market inefficiencies, and development opportunities in emerging dairy areas where milk supply falls significantly short of demand.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive technical assessment report despite metadata labeling as 'Training Manual'

classification.development_stage_evidence: The report analyzes ongoing USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS) program activities and presents findings from baseline surveys and annual monitoring surveys conducted in target counties

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on dairy value chain development
who_its_for.organizational_context: USAID implementing partners, government agencies, research institutions, and development organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Emerging dairy areas in western Kenya (Kisii, Migori, Homa Bay, Kisumu, Siaya, Kakamega, Bungoma, Busia) and lower eastern Kenya (Kitui, Makueni, Taita Taveta)

how_it_works.approach_summary: The assessment employs value chain analysis methodology to map actors, analyze productivity constraints, examine market channels, and identify intervention opportunities across input supply, production, marketing, and support services.
key_steps: Map value chain actors and their roles from input suppliers to end consumers
Analyze productivity levels and constraints by county and breed type
Examine marketing channels and calculate marketing margins
Assess support services including inputs, breeding, extension, and financial services
Evaluate institutional rules and regulatory framework
Identify collaboration opportunities and development interventions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Assessment conducted in 2018 with data from 2015-2017
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Household surveys, market data, production statistics, stakeholder interviews, secondary data from government and research institutions

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Survey methodology, value chain analysis framework, market assessment tools
what_youll_need.human_resources: Research team with expertise in livestock systems, market analysis, and survey implementation
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with local government, research institutions, and development organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive value chain assessment identifying key constraints, opportunities, and intervention points for dairy sector development
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced dairy productivity, improved market linkages, and increased incomes for smallholder farmers in target counties
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Report identifies 55% average milk supply gap across counties, with productivity ranging from 413-2760 liters per cow annually, indicating substantial room for improvement

practical_examples.description: Brookside Dairy operates organized milk collection and transportation system with farmers contracted through chilling plants
practical_examples.key_result: Demonstrates potential for formal market linkages in emerging dairy areas

practical_examples.description: Farmers depend heavily on dairy cooperatives with chilling plants, moving 79% and 55% of milk respectively
practical_examples.key_result: Shows successful cooperative-based marketing models in pre-commercial dairy areas

practical_examples.description: Most milk (62-82%) sold directly from producers to individual consumers with limited formal market participation
practical_examples.key_result: Highlights predominance of informal marketing channels and need for market development

10568-100636.limitations_and_considerations: The assessment focuses on specific counties and may not represent all emerging dairy areas in Kenya. Data gaps exist due to lack of recent livestock census (last conducted in 1988). Informal nature of milk marketing makes precise margin calculations challenging. Seasonal variations in production and market dynamics require ongoing monitoring.
key_takeaways: All 12 target counties are milk deficit areas with average supply gap of 55%, creating substantial market opportunities for increased production
Dairy productivity in target counties (683-892 liters per cow annually) is far below national average, indicating significant potential for improvement through better breeds, feeding, and management
Producer-to-consumer direct sales dominate (38% of volume), while cooperatives handle 31% and private traders 14% of marketed milk
Key intervention opportunities include promoting quality AI services, improving feeding practices, strengthening cooperatives, and developing market linkages
Support services are fragmented with multiple uncoordinated information sources leading to poor technology adoption

10568-100637.overview: This report presents a comprehensive value chain assessment of feed and fodder systems in Kenya, conducted for the USAID Kenya Crops and Dairy Market Systems (KCDMS) program. It analyzes both compound feeds (dairy meal) and fodder value chains across 11 counties, examining production, supply, demand, market channels, and opportunities for intervention to improve dairy productivity and farmer incomes.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive technical assessment report with detailed value chain analysis, despite metadata labeling it as 'Training Manual'

classification.development_stage_evidence: Based on primary data collected through field surveys conducted in March-April 2018 across eight counties, including focus group discussions and key informant interviews with various value chain actors

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on dairy value chains and livestock feed systems
who_its_for.organizational_context: USAID programs, government agencies, research institutions, NGOs, and private sector actors in agricultural development

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Mixed farming systems in emerging dairy areas including western Kenya and lower eastern regions

how_it_works.approach_summary: The assessment uses value chain analysis methodology combining qualitative and quantitative approaches, mapping actors from production to consumption, analyzing performance margins, and identifying intervention opportunities.
key_steps: Conduct focus group discussions with county livestock production staff
Interview value chain actors including feed manufacturers, traders, and farmers
Map value chains for different feed and fodder types
Analyze production costs, margins, and market performance
Estimate supply and demand for feeds and fodder
Identify constraints and opportunities for intervention
Develop recommendations for value chain development
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Field survey conducted over 6 weeks (March-April 2018)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Primary data from stakeholder interviews, secondary data from government statistics, production and cost data from value chain actors

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Value chain analysis expertise, survey design and implementation capacity, data analysis capabilities
what_youll_need.human_resources: Research team with livestock and agricultural market expertise, field enumerators, local coordinators
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnerships with county governments, access to value chain actors, coordination with local stakeholders
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive value chain maps, cost-benefit analysis of different feed types, market demand projections, identification of business opportunities
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved dairy productivity through better feed access, increased farmer incomes, strengthened feed and fodder markets
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Analysis shows positive returns from fodder production with gross margins ranging from KES 7,000-165,000 per acre, and feed miller margins of 20-44%

practical_examples.description: Small-scale feed manufacturers like Nyangusu and Angaza operate with 0.5-2 ton mixers, sourcing raw materials from Uganda and Tanzania
practical_examples.key_result: Competitive pricing with margins of 26-45% and growing demand for locally produced dairy meal

practical_examples.description: Natural pasture hay production and seed collection has become an important income source with county government support for mechanized harvesting
practical_examples.key_result: Gross margins of KES 165,100 per acre from natural pastures due to strong local and international seed markets

practical_examples.description: On-farm feed formulation is increasing with IFAD-SDCP supporting farmer groups with feed mixing equipment
practical_examples.key_result: Farmers reducing feed costs by formulating their own rations using imported raw materials at lower costs than finished feeds

10568-100637.limitations_and_considerations: High transport costs for raw materials, VAT on finished products increasing farmer costs, quality control challenges for small-scale manufacturers, seasonal availability of raw materials, limited access to credit for feed businesses, and regulatory barriers for grass seed certification.
key_takeaways: About 40% of farmers in KCDMS counties use dairy meal as feed supplement, with significant variation across counties (20% in Kitui/Makueni vs 60% in Kisii)
Potential demand for dairy meal is estimated at 46,000 MT annually, creating market opportunities worth KES 1.2-1.8 billion
Fodder production shows strong profitability with natural pastures yielding highest margins (KES 165,100/acre) due to seed sales
Small-scale feed manufacturers achieve competitive margins (20-44%) but face challenges with raw material supply and working capital
Government policy removing VAT from raw materials while taxing finished products is driving growth in informal feed formulation and ingredient trading

Conduct participatory data collection with farmers and stakeholders

Analyze results to identify gender and youth inequities

Monitor and evaluate outcomes

expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Qualitative evidence showed increased knowledge and capacity among decision-makers; 84 workshop participants reported improved understanding of tools and their applications

practical_examples.description: Extension agents began encouraging male farmers to provide land access to women for demonstrations and soybean cultivation
practical_examples.key_result: Women gained access to land and knowledge for agricultural production, with some raising vegetables and soybeans

practical_examples.description: Tools were tested with District Agricultural Extension Committees and farmer organizations
practical_examples.key_result: Participants expressed strong interest in using tools to address youth land access challenges and gender inequities

Integration with gender transformative approaches is essential for addressing root causes of inequity

10568-100644.overview: This presentation showcases CIAT's research on improved livestock feeding and tropical forages for reducing greenhouse gas emissions in West and Central Africa. It demonstrates how forage-based system intensification can achieve productivity gains while lowering environmental footprints through better feed management and improved germplasm.

resource_type.type_notes: Workshop presentation slides from Regional Awareness-Raising Workshop on Low Emissions Livestock

classification.development_stage_evidence: Research carried out with Université Evangelique en Afrique (UEA) and INERA... Testing in 3 different sites with INRAB and Université de Parakou

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers working on low emission livestock development
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, policy organizations, development agencies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Mixed crop-livestock systems in tropical environments

how_it_works.approach_summary: The LivestockPlus approach focuses on sustainable intensification through three processes: genetic improvements (improved yield, quality, stress resistance), ecological management of mixed systems, and socioeconomic optimization. Farmers adopt improved forage packages combining management practices with adequate germplasm.
key_steps: Identify and produce improved pastures resistant to extreme conditions
Integrate forage legumes and grasses into farming systems
Test germplasm in different sites and conditions
Quantify GHG reduction impacts and trade-offs
Develop context-specific solutions
Build capacity and train stakeholders
Establish science-policy partnerships
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Feed basket data (dry and wet season), survey data, expert opinion, empirical measurements

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: CLEANED environmental assessment tool for quantifying production, land requirements, GHG emissions, soil health, and water use
what_youll_need.human_resources: Research partnerships with universities and national agricultural research institutes
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Collaboration between research institutions, universities, and policy organizations

10568-100656.overview: This IWMI working paper presents a comprehensive case study analyzing the rapid adoption and impacts of motorized water pumps for agricultural irrigation in Sri Lanka. The study examines the drivers, benefits, and challenges of water-lifting technology adoption across four case study areas, with particular focus on smallholder farmers' experiences with groundwater irrigation and its effects on cropping patterns, income, and gender dynamics.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive empirical case study with mixed methods research including surveys, key informant interviews, and secondary data analysis

classification.development_stage_evidence: By 2016, the estimated number of water pumps used for agricultural purposes in the entire country increased to around 275,000

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agricultural water management
who_its_for.organizational_context: Government agencies, NGOs, donor organizations, research institutions, and water management organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Dry zone areas with seasonal rainfall patterns, small tank cascade systems, and shallow groundwater aquifers

how_it_works.approach_summary: The study employed a mixed methods approach combining secondary data analysis from customs records, key informant interviews, and detailed household surveys across four case study locations representing different irrigation contexts and groundwater use patterns.
key_steps: Data collection from Sri Lanka Customs on pump imports (2000-2016)
Key informant interviews with customs officials, private sector actors, and farmer leaders
Selection of four diverse case study locations with different irrigation practices
Structured household surveys with 198 farmers (158 pump users, 40 non-users)
Analysis of socioeconomic characteristics, hydrological features, and impacts
Assessment of enabling factors and policy interventions
Documentation of positive impacts and emerging challenges
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Field surveys and interviews conducted in October 2017, covering pump adoption trends from 2000-2017
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Import/export data, household survey data, well characteristics, crop production data, income information, and policy documentation

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Survey instruments, GPS for georeferencing, data analysis software, and access to customs/trade databases
what_youll_need.human_resources: Multidisciplinary research team including agricultural economists, hydrologists, and social scientists; trained enumerators; local translators
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Research partnerships with government agencies, customs department cooperation, and community access permissions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified in the document

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive analysis of water pump adoption trends, identification of key drivers and barriers, documentation of socioeconomic impacts, and policy recommendations
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Inform evidence-based policy decisions for sustainable agricultural water management and inclusive technology adoption
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Study documented 110% increase in pump use (2003-2015), increased cropping intensity from 100% to 300% annually, and significant income improvements for pump users

practical_examples.description: Almost all farmers in these villages adopted water pumps for cash crop cultivation, shifting from traditional rice and sesame to high-value onion and chili cultivation
practical_examples.key_result: Some farmers earn up to USD 3,975 during the Yala season by cultivating a few hectares of high-value crops

practical_examples.description: Farmers use agro-wells for conjunctive irrigation with surface water supplies, focusing on high-value crops during dry seasons
practical_examples.key_result: Up to 35% of farmers using water pumps cultivated high-value, non-paddy crops despite availability of water for rice cultivation

practical_examples.description: Farmers temporarily migrate to lease lands near rivers for groundnut cultivation using water pumps, supported by post-conflict development programs
practical_examples.key_result: Government provided 10,069 agricultural water pumps between 2009-2012 as part of post-conflict resettlement efforts

10568-100656.limitations_and_considerations: Uncontrolled groundwater extraction may create pressure on common property resources; marginalization of poor farmers who lack capital for initial investment; increased fertilizer and chemical use with potential environmental impacts; encroachment on forestlands driven by economic returns; lack of governance mechanisms for sustainable groundwater management; need for institutional measures to regulate extraction and prevent negative environmental impacts.
key_takeaways: Water pump adoption in Sri Lanka increased by 110% between 2003-2015, enabling smallholders to shift from subsistence to market-oriented agriculture
Key drivers include affordable Chinese pumps, government agro-well programs, higher profits from cash crops, and individual control over water use decisions
Pump users achieve higher incomes through increased cropping intensity (up to 300% annually), crop diversification to high-value vegetables, and expanded cultivation areas
Technology adoption creates mixed gender impacts - increased women's participation and income opportunities but also additional labor burdens
Urgent need for governance frameworks to balance promotion of inclusive technology adoption with sustainable groundwater management and environmental protection

source_and_citation.full_citation: Aheeyar, M.; Manthrithilake, H.; Ranasinghe, C.; Rengaraj, M.; Gamagedara, Y.; Barron, J. 2019. Mechanizing water lifting through pumps: a case study in Sri Lanka. Colombo, Sri Lanka: International Water Management Institute (IWMI). 61p. (IWMI Working Paper 188).

extraction_metadata.notable_issues: Some tables and figures referenced in text but content partially garbled in scraped version; comprehensive methodology and findings clearly documented

10568-100669.overview: The Potato Agri-food Systems Program is a comprehensive research-for-development initiative by the International Potato Center (CIP) that addresses agricultural development challenges through potato-focused solutions. 10568-100674.overview: This is a comprehensive academic review that synthesizes current knowledge on Fusarium wilt of banana, a devastating soil-borne disease caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense (Foc). The review addresses critical knowledge gaps in disease epidemiology and proposes integrated management approaches for sustainable disease control, with particular focus on the emerging tropical race 4 (TR4) strain that threatens global banana production.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a peer-reviewed research article published in Frontiers in Plant Science, despite being labeled as 'Framework' in metadata

classification.development_stage_evidence: This review proposes research needs and future directions, stating 'research on epidemiology-based management programs has again become a high priority, which this review proposes to address'

who_its_for.primary_audience: Plant pathologists, banana researchers, agricultural scientists, and disease management specialists
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, agricultural universities, plant protection organizations, and international agricultural research centers

countries: Global scope with specific mentions of China
where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Tropical and subtropical banana production systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: The review synthesizes existing knowledge on Fusarium wilt epidemiology, identifies research gaps, and proposes integrated management strategies combining exclusion, resistant cultivars, soil health practices, and biological control approaches.
key_steps: Pathogen exclusion and quarantine measures
Early detection and plant eradication
Use of resistant or tolerant cultivars when available
Soil health management through organic amendments and cover crops
Application of beneficial microorganisms
Integrated cultural practices including proper nutrition management
Continuous monitoring and containment strategies
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Long-term management approach spanning multiple cropping cycles
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Soil analysis, pathogen diagnostic tools, disease monitoring data, cultivar resistance information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Diagnostic tools for pathogen detection, soil testing capabilities, access to resistant planting material
what_youll_need.human_resources: Trained field workers for disease identification, plant protection officers, extension workers, researchers
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Plant protection organizations, research institutions, certification agencies, farmer organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Variable depending on scale and management intensity - from basic biosecurity measures to comprehensive integrated management programs

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Reduced disease incidence, improved soil health, enhanced plant resistance, better pathogen containment
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Sustainable banana production, reduced crop losses, improved food security and livelihoods for banana-dependent communities
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Studies show 20% reduction in disease intensity with cover crops, up to 97% reduction with crop rotation using Chinese leek, and significant improvements with bioorganic fertilizers

practical_examples.description: Development of Giant Cavendish Tissue Culture Variants (GCTCV) with intermediate resistance to Foc TR4
practical_examples.key_result: GCTCV-118, GCTCV-119, GCTCV-218, and GCTCV-219 are being used commercially to mitigate TR4 losses

practical_examples.description: Crop rotation with Chinese leek (Allium tuberosum) for Fusarium wilt control
practical_examples.key_result: Up to 97% reduction in disease incidence through antifungal volatiles released by the rotation crop

practical_examples.description: Integrated management of Prata-type cultivars combining resistance with cultural practices
practical_examples.key_result: Growers can cope with the disease and make banana crop profitable despite susceptibility to Fusarium wilt

practical_examples.description: Use of Pinto peanut (Arachis pintoi) as ground cover in banana plantations
practical_examples.key_result: 20% reduction in Fusarium wilt intensity and increased bunch weight in second crop cycle

10568-100674.limitations_and_considerations: The review highlights that no single management approach is completely effective against Fusarium wilt. Success depends on integrated approaches that consider local conditions, pathogen strains, cultivar resistance levels, and environmental factors. The long survival time of the pathogen in soil (>20 years) makes eradication extremely difficult. Climate change and global trade continue to facilitate pathogen spread.
key_takeaways: Fusarium wilt tropical race 4 (TR4) poses a major threat to global banana production, having spread from Asia to Africa and the Middle East since 2010
Integrated management combining exclusion, resistant cultivars, soil health practices, and biological control is more effective than single approaches
Soil suppressiveness can be enhanced through organic amendments, beneficial microorganisms, and proper nutrient management, particularly avoiding ammonium-based nitrogen sources
Early detection, rapid response, and strict biosecurity measures are critical for preventing pathogen introduction and spread
Research gaps remain in understanding pathogen survival in alternative hosts, dispersal mechanisms, and microbiome interactions that could inform better management strategies

10568-100675.overview: The PoSHAN surveys represent a nationally representative, multi-year research platform designed to understand the complex pathways linking agriculture to nutrition outcomes in Nepal. This framework provides a systematic approach for collecting annual data on markets, household food security, dietary patterns, and nutritional status to inform policy and program interventions.

resource_type.type_notes: While published as an academic paper, the content describes a comprehensive survey framework and methodology for understanding agriculture-to-nutrition pathways

classification.development_stage_evidence: Data collection for three of the annual surveys was completed from May to September in 2013, 2014, and 2016... The number of households visited and screened ranged from a low of 6,687 in 2015 to a high of 12,143 in 2016

who_its_for.primary_audience: Development practitioners, policymakers, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders working on agriculture-nutrition linkages
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, government agencies, international development organizations, and policy-making bodies

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Designed to represent major agroecological zones (Mountains, Hills, and Terai plains)

how_it_works.approach_summary: The framework employs annual, same-season surveys of nationally representative households with preschool children, combined with seasonal assessments in sentinel sites. It uses a mixed longitudinal design to track households over time while continuously adding newly eligible households.
key_steps: Stratify country by major agroecological zones and systematically sample VDCs
Conduct district official interviews and VDC focus groups
Perform ward enumeration to identify all eligible households
Collect market survey data on food and agricultural input prices
Conduct household interviews on socioeconomic status and agricultural practices
Interview women and children on dietary intake, health, and nutrition
Perform anthropometric measurements and hemoglobin testing
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Annual surveys conducted from mid-May to early September, with additional seasonal assessments in sentinel sites
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Trained field teams, standardized questionnaires, anthropometric equipment, hemoglobin testing devices, and data management systems

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Digital scales, height-length boards, MUAC tapes, hemoglobinometers, GPS devices, data entry systems (FoxPro, SQL server)
what_youll_need.human_resources: 21 field teams of 3 members each, team leaders, quality control teams, data entry operators, supervisors, and research coordinators
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Ethical approvals from national health research councils, partnerships with local research organizations, government permissions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Multi-year funding for field teams, equipment, training, data management, and analysis (USAID-sponsored in Nepal case)

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Annual reports with zone-specific and national estimates of nutritional status, dietary intake, food security, agricultural productivity, and participation in interventions
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Enhanced understanding of agriculture-to-nutrition pathways to inform policy and program design for improving food security and nutritional outcomes
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Successfully collected data from ~4,286-5,097 households annually across 21 VDCs, with 97-99% consent rates and comprehensive longitudinal tracking

practical_examples.description: Four-year implementation (2013-2016) covering 21 VDCs across three agroecological zones with annual surveys and seasonal assessments in sentinel sites.

10568-100675.limitations_and_considerations: Implementation can be disrupted by natural disasters (2015 earthquake limited data collection). Quality control is challenging across remote, geographically dispersed communities with multiple ethnic and caste groups.
key_takeaways: Nationally representative, longitudinal design enables both cross-sectional and temporal analysis of agriculture-nutrition pathways
Same-season annual surveys are essential for minimizing seasonal variability and enabling year-to-year comparisons
Agroecological stratification is vital in countries with diverse farming systems and environmental conditions
Seasonal assessments in sentinel sites provide crucial insights into timing of interventions to mitigate food shortages
Rigorous training, standardization, and quality control procedures are paramount for large-scale, multi-team data collection

practical_examples.location: Nepal Terai districts (Sunsari, Morang, Rupandehi, Banke, Kailali)
practical_examples.description: Pre-release seed multiplication by private companies starting in 2007/8 before official release in 2010, with Kalika Seed Company multiplying 40kg of breeder seed
practical_examples.key_result: About 44 tons of source seed produced before release, enabling rapid dissemination

10568-100676.limitations_and_considerations: Gap between seed production potential and actual adoption - while estimated C1 seed could cover 56.88% of Terai wheat area, actual share was only 22%. Inefficiency in seed delivery systems with less than 20% of farmer seed requirements met through formal channels. Higher profit margins in vegetables and hybrid rice may reduce agro-vet focus on wheat seeds.
key_takeaways: Pre-release seed multiplication by private companies significantly accelerates variety adoption and commercialization
Dealership with national seed companies, perceived variety performance, and total wheat seed sales volume are key factors driving commercialization
Decentralization of foundation seed production to private actors while maintaining public sector focus on breeder seed can improve resource efficiency
Close coordination between public research, private seed companies, and community organizations is essential for effective seed delivery
Awareness programs comparing new varieties with existing popular varieties are crucial for rapid adoption

source_and_citation.full_citation: Timsina, K.P., Ghimire, Y.N., Gauchan, D., Subedi, S., & Adhikari, S.P. (2018). Lessons for promotion of new agricultural technology: a case of Vijay wheat variety in Nepal. Agriculture & Food Security, 7, 63.

10568-100686.overview: This is a comprehensive research report by the EQUALS Research Group that surveys the global landscape of gender equality in digital access, skills, and leadership. It provides preliminary findings on gender digital divides across three core areas: ICT access, advanced digital skills development, and participation in ICT industry leadership roles.

resource_type.type_notes: Comprehensive research report with data analysis, case studies, and recommendations

classification.development_stage_evidence: Report analyzes existing global data and research across multiple countries and regions, indicating widespread implementation of various gender digital equality initiatives

who_its_for.primary_audience: Policymakers, practitioners, researchers, and CGIAR stakeholders interested in addressing gender inequality in digital technologies
who_its_for.organizational_context: Development organizations, government agencies, research institutions, private sector, and international partnerships

countries: Global coverage with specific data from 72+ countries including Bangladesh, India, Pakistan, South Africa, Colombia, Peru, Argentina, Finland, Turkey, Saudi Arabia
where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Rural and urban contexts with emphasis on agricultural information systems

how_it_works.approach_summary: The report conducts systematic analysis of existing research, official statistics, and case studies across three action areas: ICT access, skills development, and leadership participation. It identifies barriers, assesses data availability, and provides evidence-based recommendations.
key_steps: Review official statistics and academic literature on gender digital divides
Analyze sex-disaggregated data on ICT access, skills, and leadership
Identify barriers to gender equality across the three action areas
Assess availability and quality of gender-disaggregated data
Document case studies and best practices
Develop recommendations for addressing identified gaps
Provide framework for future research and policy interventions
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: This is a preliminary report with final version planned for 2019
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Sex-disaggregated ICT statistics, survey data, academic research, policy documents, and case study information

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Access to international databases (ITU, World Bank, UNESCO), statistical analysis capabilities
what_youll_need.human_resources: Research team with expertise in gender studies, ICT policy, data analysis, and development
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Partnership with international organizations, government agencies, and research institutions
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Not specified, but involves multi-country data collection and analysis

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: Comprehensive analysis of gender digital divides, identification of barriers and recommendations, assessment of data gaps, case studies of successful interventions
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Inform policy and programming decisions to reduce gender digital inequalities, guide future research priorities, support evidence-based interventions
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Report documents various successful initiatives like GSMA Connected Women Initiative reaching 10 million new women with mobile services, and SheTrades supporting 250+ women-owned enterprises

practical_examples.description: GSMA Connected Women Initiative with 36 mobile operators making commitments to reduce gender gaps in mobile internet and money services
practical_examples.key_result: Reached over 10 million new women with mobile money or mobile internet services

practical_examples.description: SheTrades Initiative providing digital skills training to women entrepreneurs in IT and IT-enabled services
practical_examples.key_result: Supported 250+ women-owned enterprises with $2.3 million in sales commitments across 42 countries

practical_examples.description: Qualitative research on youth ICT use examining gender differences in online privacy and violence experiences
practical_examples.key_result: Identified gender-specific patterns in digital privacy management and exposure to online violence

10568-100686.limitations_and_considerations: Limited internationally comparable sex-disaggregated data especially for developing countries; most research from North America and Europe; contradictory findings across different contexts; definitional and methodological challenges in measuring gender digital equality; lack of longitudinal data for trend analysis; insufficient research on intersectional identities beyond binary gender categories.
key_takeaways: Gender gaps exist across most aspects of ICT access, skills, and leadership, but patterns vary significantly by region and country context
Barriers to gender digital equality include infrastructure availability, financial constraints, ability/aptitude issues, perceived relevance, safety concerns, and socio-cultural contexts
Women are underrepresented in advanced digital skills, ICT employment, and technology leadership positions globally
Digital technologies can both empower women and expose them to new forms of violence and discrimination
Addressing gender digital inequality requires multi-sectoral coordination beyond telecommunications, including education, employment, and social policy interventions

source_and_citation.full_citation: Huyer, Sophia (2018). ICT for food security in a changing climate: A path to women's empowerment. EQUALS Research Group.

The document provides detailed requirements, assessment criteria, and timelines for CGIAR Centers to submit proposals for hosting and managing this platform to strengthen gender integration across CGIAR research programs.

resource_type.type_notes: This is a formal call for proposals document with specific requirements, templates, and procedures for submission

classification.development_stage_evidence: This is a call for proposals to establish a new platform, indicating it is in the conceptual/planning stage

who_its_for.primary_audience: CGIAR Centers and their leadership
who_its_for.organizational_context: International agricultural research centers within the CGIAR system

Proposals are assessed by external experts moderated by the Independent Science for Development Council.
key_steps: Centers prepare proposals following detailed template requirements
Submit proposals by July 2, 2019 deadline with cover letter, full proposal, and budget
External independent assessment process moderated by ISDC
Possible re-submission period if required
System Management Board review and endorsement recommendation
System Council consideration and approval

10568-100702.overview: This PhD thesis investigates the feasibility and potential benefits of across-country genetic evaluations for dairy cattle in sub-Saharan Africa, using performance data from Holstein-Friesian and Jersey breeds in Kenya, South Africa, and Zimbabwe. The research addresses the challenge of limited genetic improvement in African dairy cattle due to poor data recording, small herd sizes, and lack of systematic breeding programs.

resource_type.type_notes: PhD thesis with comprehensive research on dairy breeding strategies

classification.development_stage_evidence: This thesis set out to assess... the possibility of developing international genetic evaluations of the foreign dairy breeds used in dairy production in several countries in sub-Saharan Africa

who_its_for.primary_audience: Scientists, researchers, and dairy breeding specialists
who_its_for.organizational_context: Research institutions, universities, agricultural research centers, and breeding organizations

where_it_applies.agro_ecological_context: Various dairy production systems from small-scale to large-scale intensive and extensive farming

how_it_works.approach_summary: The research pooled dairy performance and pedigree data from three countries to conduct joint genetic evaluations, comparing within-country versus across-country genetic parameter estimation and breeding value prediction for Holstein-Friesian and Jersey cattle.
key_steps: Conduct stakeholder survey on dairy improvement infrastructure
Collect and edit performance data from multiple countries
Identify genetic links through common sires across countries
Estimate genetic parameters within and across countries
Calculate breeding values and reliabilities
Predict genetic gains from different selection strategies
Compare benefits of joint versus national evaluations
how_it_works.duration_or_timeline: Multi-year research project (PhD thesis completed in 2018)
how_it_works.data_or_inputs_required: Animal performance records (milk yield, reproduction traits), pedigree information, herd management data, and international sire identities

what_youll_need.technical_requirements: Statistical genetics software (R, ASReml), data management systems, and genetic evaluation methodologies
what_youll_need.human_resources: Geneticists, statisticians, data managers, and collaboration between countries
what_youll_need.institutional_requirements: Data sharing agreements between countries, standardized recording systems, and coordinated breeding organizations
what_youll_need.estimated_resources: Significant investment in data infrastructure, training, and international collaboration frameworks

expected_outcomes.direct_outputs: More accurate genetic parameters, improved breeding value estimates, and enhanced genetic gain predictions
expected_outcomes.intended_impact: Improved dairy cattle productivity through better genetic selection and enhanced breeding programs across sub-Saharan Africa
expected_outcomes.evidence_of_effectiveness: Results showed that all three countries would benefit from genetic progress generated from selection in an across country initiative. For production traits, Kenya benefited the most (100% increase in genetic gain from across-country compared to within-country selection) than Zimbabwe (55-73% increase over within-country) and lowest benefits for South Africa (2-28%)

practical_examples.description: Joint genetic evaluation of Holstein-Friesian and Jersey breeds using pooled performance data from three countries
practical_examples.key_result: Kenya achieved 100% increase in genetic gain compared to within-country selection, while Zimbabwe gained 55-73% and South Africa 2-28%

10568-100702.limitations_and_considerations: Poor data quality, lack of international sire identities, inadequate performance recording systems, small herd sizes, and limited genetic links between countries. The study also notes that generation intervals and genomic selection technologies were not fully addressed.
key_takeaways: Across-country genetic evaluations provide more accurate genetic parameters than within-country analyses for dairy cattle in sub-Saharan Africa
Countries with limited data benefit most from joint evaluations, while those with established programs can serve as genetic material hubs
Systematic data recording and international collaboration are essential prerequisites for successful joint breeding programs
Joint evaluations could accelerate genetic progress and provide wider choice of animals for selection than national programs alone
Harmonized data collection methods and trait definitions across countries are crucial for effective across-country genetic evaluations

source_and_citation.full_citation: Opoola, O. (2018). Across country dairy breeding strategies in sub-Saharan Africa. PhD thesis, University of Edinburgh.

10568-100702._title_meta: Across country dairy breeding strategies in sub-Saharan Africa
